abstract: Autoregressive visual generation models typically rely on tokenizers to compress images into tokens that can be predicted sequentially. A fundamental dilemma exists in token representation: discrete tokens enable straightforward modeling with standard cross-entropy loss, but suffer from information loss and tokenizer training instability; continuous tokens better preserve visual details, but require complex distribution modeling, complicating the generation pipeline. In this paper, we propose TokenBridge, which bridges this gap by maintaining the strong representation capacity of continuous tokens while preserving the modeling simplicity of discrete tokens. To achieve this, we decouple discretization from the tokenizer training process through post-training quantization that directly obtains discrete tokens from continuous representations. Specifically, we introduce a dimension-wise quantization strategy that independently discretizes each feature dimension, paired with a lightweight autoregressive prediction mechanism that efficiently model the resulting large token space. Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves reconstruction and generation quality on par with continuous methods while using standard categorical prediction. This work demonstrates that bridging discrete and continuous paradigms can effectively harness the strengths of both approaches, providing a promising direction for high-quality visual generation with simple autoregressive modeling. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: High-fidelity visual generation from continuous VAE latents can be achieved with simple categorical prediction (using cross-entropy loss) through judicious post-training discretization, challenging the necessity of complex continuous distribution models.

Modeling inter-channel dependencies using a dimension-wise autoregressive approach is crucial for high-quality visual generation, as simple parallel prediction leads to significantly degraded results.

The inherent properties of VAE latent spaces (bounded range, near-Gaussian distribution) enable robust and effective training-free dimension-wise quantization, which generalizes across various autoencoder architectures.
originalProblem: A fundamental trade-off exists in autoregressive visual generation between preserving high visual fidelity (often achieved with continuous tokens) and maintaining modeling simplicity and efficiency (characteristic of discrete tokens).

Traditional discrete tokenizers (e.g., VQ-VAEs) suffer from information loss, training instability due to non-differentiable quantization, and a difficult trade-off between vocabulary size and expressiveness.

Autoregressive generation with continuous tokens typically requires complex distribution modeling techniques (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models or diffusion models), increasing architectural complexity and specialized training procedures.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art FID of 1.55 and IS of 313.3 on the ImageNet 256x256 benchmark (Ours-H), matching the FID of MAR-H while achieving higher IS and Recall with comparable parameters.
Demonstrated reconstruction quality comparable to continuous VAEs (rFID = 1.11, IS = 305.4 with B=64), significantly surpassing traditional discrete tokenizers.
Provided a 5.94x speedup in inference compared to diffusion-based token prediction methods and enables unique confidence-guided generation capabilities.
solution: Employs a post-training, training-free dimension-wise quantization strategy to convert continuous VAE latent features into discrete tokens, leveraging the VAE's ability to capture rich visual information without discrete tokenizer training complexities.

Introduces a lightweight autoregressive head that factorizes the prediction of high-dimensional discrete tokens into a sequence of channel-wise predictions, efficiently modeling inter-channel dependencies in large vocabulary spaces.
Optimizes the channel prediction order based on frequency characteristics (prioritizing low-frequency information) to improve generation coherence and quality.
paper_summary.summary: TokenBridge, a collaboration between the University of Hong Kong, ByteDance Seed, École Polytechnique, and Peking University, proposes a method for autoregressive visual generation that discretizes pre-trained continuous VAE latent features after training. It achieves an ImageNet 256x256 FID of 1.55, matching or surpassing complex diffusion-based token prediction methods while demonstrating a 5.94x faster inference speed.

title: Bridging Continuous and Discrete Tokens for Autoregressive Visual Generation

abstract: In autoregressive (AR) image generation, visual tokenizers compress images into compact discrete latent tokens, enabling efficient training of downstream autoregressive models for visual generation via next-token prediction. While scaling visual tokenizers improves image reconstruction quality, it often degrades downstream generation quality -- a challenge not adequately addressed in existing literature. To address this, we introduce GigaTok, the first approach to simultaneously improve image reconstruction, generation, and representation learning when scaling visual tokenizers. We identify the growing complexity of latent space as the key factor behind the reconstruction vs. generation dilemma. To mitigate this, we propose semantic regularization, which aligns tokenizer features with semantically consistent features from a pre-trained visual encoder. This constraint prevents excessive latent space complexity during scaling, yielding consistent improvements in both reconstruction and downstream autoregressive generation. Building on semantic regularization, we explore three key practices for scaling tokenizers:(1) using 1D tokenizers for better scalability, (2) prioritizing decoder scaling when expanding both encoder and decoder, and (3) employing entropy loss to stabilize training for billion-scale tokenizers. By scaling to $\bf{3 \space billion}$ parameters, GigaTok achieves state-of-the-art performance in reconstruction, downstream AR generation, and downstream AR representation quality.

keyInsights: The "reconstruction vs. generation dilemma" arises because naively scaled tokenizers encode excessive low-level details, creating an overly complex and semantically inconsistent latent space.

Semantic regularization effectively resolves this dilemma, enabling simultaneous improvements in image reconstruction, downstream autoregressive generation, and learned representation quality as tokenizers scale.

Optimal scaling strategies involve an asymmetric design that prioritizes the decoder and leverages 1D tokenizers with entropy loss, which are crucial for achieving convergence and high performance in very large models.
originalProblem: Visual tokenizers faced a "reconstruction vs. generation dilemma," where scaling for better image reconstruction paradoxically degraded downstream autoregressive generation quality.
Naively scaled tokenizers often produced overly complex latent spaces, making it difficult for downstream AR models to learn token dependencies effectively.
The impact of scaling visual tokenizer parameter counts on overall generative performance had not been thoroughly investigated, despite its importance for AR image generation.
results: The 2.9B GigaTok tokenizer achieved state-of-the-art reconstruction (0.79 rFID, 0.1947 LPIPS) on ImageNet 256x256 among discrete tokenizers.
When paired with a 1.4B LlamaGen AR model, the 2.9B GigaTok tokenizer achieved a state-of-the-art gFID of 1.98 for autoregressive next-token-prediction models.
The 1.4B AR model trained on GigaTok's tokens also demonstrated state-of-the-art linear probing accuracy (74.0%), indicating the learning of superior visual representations.
solution: Introduced "semantic regularization," which aligns the tokenizer's decoder features with semantically consistent features from a pre-trained visual encoder (DINOv2-B) to simplify the latent space.
Employed a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture, favoring 1D tokenizers with Q-Formers due to their superior scalability and reduced inductive bias compared to 2D tokenizers.
Implemented key scaling practices, including prioritizing decoder parameter scaling, utilizing 1D tokenizers, and incorporating entropy loss to stabilize training for billion-scale models.
paper_summary.summary: GigaTok introduces a method for scaling visual tokenizers to 3 billion parameters, successfully addressing the "reconstruction vs. generation dilemma" in autoregressive image generation. This approach yields state-of-the-art performance, including a gFID of 1.98 for 1.4B AR models and improved representation quality on ImageNet.

title: GigaTok: Scaling Visual Tokenizers to 3 Billion Parameters for Autoregressive Image Generation

abstract: Since the advent of popular visual generation frameworks like VQGAN and latent diffusion models, state-of-the-art image generation systems have generally been two-stage systems that first tokenize or compress visual data into a lower-dimensional latent space before learning a generative model. Tokenizer training typically follows a standard recipe in which images are compressed and reconstructed subject to a combination of MSE, perceptual, and adversarial losses. Diffusion autoencoders have been proposed in prior work as a way to learn end-to-end perceptually-oriented image compression, but have not yet shown state-of-the-art performance on the competitive task of ImageNet-1K reconstruction. We propose FlowMo, a transformer-based diffusion autoencoder that achieves a new state-of-the-art for image tokenization at multiple compression rates without using convolutions, adversarial losses, spatially-aligned two-dimensional latent codes, or distilling from other tokenizers. Our key insight is that FlowMo training should be broken into a mode-matching pre-training stage and a mode-seeking post-training stage. In addition, we conduct extensive analyses and explore the training of generative models atop the FlowMo tokenizer. Our code and models will be available at this http URL .

keyInsights: Diffusion models provide a stable and robust mechanism for conditional image generation, offering an advantage over adversarial losses in tokenization.

For optimal perceptual reconstruction, it is more effective to explicitly guide the diffusion autoencoder to sample modes of the reconstruction distribution that are perceptually closest to the original image.

A post-training objective that backpropagates perceptual loss through the entire multi-step diffusion sampling chain is essential for achieving superior mode-seeking behavior and high perceptual quality.
originalProblem: Existing image tokenizers often rely on unstable adversarial losses and convolutional architectures, which are difficult to tune and don't fully leverage transformer scalability.
Many state-of-the-art tokenizers require distillation from pre-existing CNN-based models, adding complexity and architectural dependencies.
Previous diffusion autoencoder approaches had not yet achieved state-of-the-art perceptual reconstruction performance on competitive benchmarks like ImageNet-1K.
results: FlowMo achieved new state-of-the-art perceptual image tokenization on ImageNet-1K at 256x256 resolution, with FlowMo-Lo scoring an rFID of 0.95 (0.070 BPP) and FlowMo-Hi achieving an rFID of 0.56 (0.219 BPP).
These leading results were obtained using a fully transformer-based architecture, without convolutions, adversarial losses, spatially-aligned 2D latent codes, or distillation from other tokenizers.
Downstream MaskGiT generative models trained on FlowMo's discrete latent space demonstrated competitive image generation quality, confirming the suitability of the tokenized representations.
solution: A transformer-based diffusion autoencoder, named FlowMo, is introduced, employing an encoder and a decoder built on the Multimodal Diffusion Image Transformer (MMDiT) architecture.

A two-stage training scheme is implemented, beginning with mode-matching pre-training using rectified flow and perceptual losses, followed by mode-seeking post-training to optimize for perceptually close reconstructions.
Continuous latent codes from the encoder are binarized using Lookup-Free Quantization (LFQ) to create a discrete, 1-dimensional latent space.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Stanford University and the University of Michigan developed FlowMo, a transformer-based diffusion autoencoder with a mode-seeking training strategy, for image tokenization. This approach sets a new state-of-the-art for perceptual image reconstruction on ImageNet-1K, with FlowMo-Lo achieving an rFID of 0.95 at 0.070 BPP and FlowMo-Hi reaching 0.56 rFID at 0.219 BPP, without relying on convolutions or adversarial losses.

title: Flow to the Mode: Mode-Seeking Diffusion Autoencoders for State-of-the-Art Image Tokenization

abstract: Autoencoder (AE) is the key to the success of latent diffusion models for image and video generation, reducing the denoising resolution and improving efficiency. However, the power of AE has long been underexplored in terms of network design, compression ratio, and training strategy. In this work, we systematically examine the architecture design choices and optimize the computation distribution to obtain a series of efficient and high-compression video AEs that can decode in real time even on mobile devices. We also propose an omni-training objective to unify the design of plain Autoencoder and image-conditioned I2V VAE, achieving multifunctionality in a single VAE network but with enhanced quality. In addition, we propose a novel latent consistency loss that provides stable improvements in reconstruction quality. Latent consistency loss outperforms prior auxiliary losses including LPIPS, GAN and DWT in terms of both quality improvements and simplicity. H3AE achieves ultra-high compression ratios and real-time decoding speed on GPU and mobile, and outperforms prior arts in terms of reconstruction metrics by a large margin. We finally validate our AE by training a DiT on its latent space and demonstrate fast, high-quality text-to-video generation capability.

keyInsights: Strategic architectural choices, including the use of 2D convolutions for high-resolution spatial stages and 3D causal attention at the bottleneck, are essential for efficient, high-quality video autoencoding.

An "omni-objective" training strategy, where first-frame features are randomly introduced as conditions, unifies Text-to-Video and Image-to-Video generation in a single VAE while also enhancing overall reconstruction quality.

The Latent Consistency Loss, which reuses the VAE encoder to enforce consistency between true and reconstructed latents, offers a stable and effective method for improving VAE reconstruction quality without common auxiliary loss drawbacks.
originalProblem: Video autoencoders (VAEs) in latent diffusion models for video generation have been underexplored, lacking systematic optimization in design, compression, and training strategies.
Existing VAEs struggle to achieve ultra-high compression ratios while maintaining high reconstruction quality, creating a computational bottleneck for video diffusion models.
There is a need for VAEs capable of real-time decoding, especially on resource-constrained platforms like mobile devices, and for unified support of various generation tasks.
results: H3AE variants consistently achieve superior reconstruction quality (e.g., +1.76 PSNR, +0.0333 SSIM for 4x16x16 variant over Cosmos-Tokenizer 4x8x8) and ultra-high compression ratios (up to 8x64x64) compared to state-of-the-art video autoencoders.

It demonstrates exceptional efficiency, with the 8x32x32 variant having half the parameters of LTX-VAE and achieving a 2.5x speedup on iPhone, enabling real-time video decoding even on mobile devices.

A Diffusion Transformer trained on H3AE's latent space shows improved VBench scores and significantly faster generation speeds, achieving a 4x speedup on iPhone for 17-frame 512x512 video generation compared to an 8x32x32 H3AE-based DiT.
solution: H3AE employs a systematically optimized architecture using 2D spatial convolutions for high-resolution stages, 3D causal attention at the bottleneck for temporal modeling, PixelShuffle for upsampling, and PixelNorm for mobile efficiency.

An "omni-objective training" method allows a single VAE to support both text-to-video (T2V) and image-to-video (I2V) generation by randomly injecting first-frame features as a condition during training.

A novel "Latent Consistency Loss" (LC Loss) reuses the VAE encoder to enforce consistency between original and reconstructed latent representations, thereby improving reconstruction quality and training stability.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Snap Inc. and Northeastern University developed H3AE, a high compression, high speed, and high quality autoencoder designed for video diffusion models. This framework systematically optimizes architectural design and training strategies to achieve ultra-high compression ratios and real-time decoding speeds on mobile devices, while significantly improving video reconstruction fidelity and unifying support for various generation tasks.

title: H3AE: High Compression, High Speed, and High Quality AutoEncoder for Video Diffusion Models

abstract: The quality of the latent space in visual tokenizers (e.g., VAEs) is crucial for modern generative models. However, the standard reconstruction-based training paradigm produces a latent space that is biased towards low-level information, leading to a foundation flaw: better pixel-level accuracy does not lead to higher-quality generation. This implies that pouring extensive compute into visual tokenizer pre-training translates poorly to improved performance in generation. We identify this as the ``pre-training scaling problem`` and suggest a necessary shift: to be effective for generation, a latent space must concisely represent high-level semantics. We present VTP, a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework, pioneering the joint optimization of image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction losses. Our large-scale study reveals two principal findings: (1) understanding is a key driver of generation, and (2) much better scaling properties, where generative performance scales effectively with compute, parameters, and data allocated to the pretraining of the visual tokenizer. After large-scale pre-training, our tokenizer delivers a competitive profile (78.2 zero-shot accuracy and 0.36 rFID on ImageNet) and 4.1 times faster convergence on generation compared to advanced distillation methods. More importantly, it scales effectively: without modifying standard DiT training specs, solely investing more FLOPS in pretraining VTP achieves 65.8\% FID improvement in downstream generation, while conventional autoencoder stagnates very early at 1/10 FLOPS. Our pre-trained models are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: Towards Scalable Pre-training of Visual Tokenizers for Generation

abstract: In this work, we present a novel direction to build an image tokenizer directly on top of a frozen vision foundation model, which is a largely underexplored area. Specifically, we employ a frozen vision foundation model as the encoder of our tokenizer. To enhance its effectiveness, we introduce two key components: (1) a region-adaptive quantization framework that reduces redundancy in the pre-trained features on regular 2D grids, and (2) a semantic reconstruction objective that aligns the tokenizer's outputs with the foundation model's representations to preserve semantic fidelity. Based on these designs, our proposed image tokenizer, VFMTok, achieves substantial improvements in image reconstruction and generation quality, while also enhancing token efficiency. It further boosts autoregressive (AR) generation -- achieving a gFID of 1.36 on ImageNet benchmarks, while accelerating model convergence by three times, and enabling high-fidelity class-conditional synthesis without the need for classifier-free guidance (CFG). The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Pre-trained, frozen Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) can effectively function as robust encoders for image tokenizers, directly contributing high-level semantic features.
Region-adaptive tokenization, implemented via deformable attention, allows for a compact representation (256 tokens) without sacrificing reconstruction or generation quality, addressing redundancy.
Enforcing reconstruction of the VFM's own features during tokenizer training is vital for embedding and preserving rich semantic information in the discrete latent space.
originalProblem: Existing image tokenizers for autoregressive generation, like VQGAN, often produce low-semantic fidelity and redundant visual tokens.
Training these tokenizers from scratch is computationally intensive and leads to a lack of rich, high-level semantic information in their latent spaces.
The inefficiency of current tokenizers results in prolonged training times for downstream autoregressive models and requires classifier-free guidance, increasing inference costs.
results: VFMTok achieves efficient image reconstruction, representing images with 256 tokens (compared to 576-1024 for baselines) while maintaining strong quality (rFID 0.89, rIS 215.4).
When integrated with autoregressive models, VFMTok enables competitive image generation, achieving a gFID of 1.36 on ImageNet 256x256, and consistently outperforming LlamaGen models.

Autoregressive models trained with VFMTok tokens converge three times faster and support high-fidelity class-conditional generation without classifier-free guidance, leading to reduced inference time.
solution: Utilize a frozen, pre-trained Vision Foundation Model (VFM) as a multi-level encoder to directly leverage its semantically rich features.
Develop a region-adaptive token generation mechanism using learnable anchor queries and deformable attention to efficiently extract compact, semantically coherent tokens.
Introduce a dual reconstruction objective, aiming to reconstruct both the original image and the VFM's high-level features, ensuring semantic integrity of the generated tokens.
paper_summary.summary: The paper introduces VFMTok, an image tokenizer that leverages frozen vision foundation models (VFMs) and a region-adaptive quantization strategy to create semantically rich and compact visual tokens. This approach enables autoregressive image generation models to achieve competitive performance (e.g., a gFID of 1.36 on ImageNet 256x256), while also speeding up training convergence by 3x and supporting high-fidelity generation without classifier-free guidance.

title: Vision Foundation Models as Effective Visual Tokenizers for Autoregressive Image Generation

abstract: Discrete visual tokenizers transform images into a sequence of tokens, enabling token-based visual generation akin to language models. However, this process is inherently challenging, as it requires both compressing visual signals into a compact representation and discretizing them into a fixed set of codes. Traditional discrete tokenizers typically learn the two tasks jointly, often leading to unstable training, low codebook utilization, and limited reconstruction quality. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{CODA}(\textbf{CO}ntinuous-to-\textbf{D}iscrete \textbf{A}daptation), a framework that decouples compression and discretization. Instead of training discrete tokenizers from scratch, CODA adapts off-the-shelf continuous VAEs -- already optimized for perceptual compression -- into discrete tokenizers via a carefully designed discretization process. By primarily focusing on discretization, CODA ensures stable and efficient training while retaining the strong visual fidelity of continuous VAEs. Empirically, with $\mathbf{6 \times}$ less training budget than standard VQGAN, our approach achieves a remarkable codebook utilization of 100% and notable reconstruction FID (rFID) of $\mathbf{0.43}$ and $\mathbf{1.34}$ for $8 \times$ and $16 \times$ compression on ImageNet 256$\times$ 256 benchmark.
title: CODA: Repurposing Continuous VAEs for Discrete Tokenization

abstract: We introduce a novel visual tokenization framework that embeds a provable PCA-like structure into the latent token space. While existing visual tokenizers primarily optimize for reconstruction fidelity, they often neglect the structural properties of the latent space--a critical factor for both interpretability and downstream tasks. Our method generates a 1D causal token sequence for images, where each successive token contributes non-overlapping information with mathematically guaranteed decreasing explained variance, analogous to principal component analysis. This structural constraint ensures the tokenizer extracts the most salient visual features first, with each subsequent token adding diminishing yet complementary information. Additionally, we identified and resolved a semantic-spectrum coupling effect that causes the unwanted entanglement of high-level semantic content and low-level spectral details in the tokens by leveraging a diffusion decoder. Experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction performance and enables better interpretability to align with the human vision system. Moreover, autoregressive models trained on our token sequences achieve performance comparable to current state-of-the-art methods while requiring fewer tokens for training and inference.

keyInsights: A diffusion decoder's spectral autoregressive process can effectively handle low-level spectral reconstruction, allowing visual tokens to focus purely on high-level semantic content.
Intermittently replacing later tokens with null conditions during training (nested CFG) can formally enforce an orthogonal, variance-decaying (PCA-like) structure in a deep latent space.
Structuring visual tokens to align with human visual perception's global precedence effect, where global features are processed before local details, enhances interpretability and efficiency.
originalProblem: Modern visual tokenizers lack structural properties like orthogonality and orderliness in their latent spaces, making them less interpretable.
Existing tokenizers suffer from "semantic-spectrum coupling," where high-level semantic content and low-level spectral details are entangled within latent codes.
Unstructured latent spaces hinder interpretability and can reduce efficiency for downstream generative tasks, particularly autoregressive modeling.
results: SEMANTICIST achieved an rFID score of 0.72 on ImageNet reconstruction with a DiT-XL decoder, surpassing previous state-of-the-art tokenizers like ImageFolder (0.80 rFID).

The autoregressive model, ",epsil"LlamaGen, trained on SEMANTICIST's tokens obtained a competitive generative FID (2.57) using only 32 tokens, significantly fewer than other AR models (e.g., ImageFolder's 286 tokens).

Linear probing experiments confirmed a PCA-like structure, with early tokens capturing up to 63.5% top-1 classification accuracy for category-level information and successfully decoupling semantic and spectral content.
solution: SEMANTICIST combines a Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder with a conditional Diffusion-Transformer (DiT) decoder operating in the latent space of a pre-trained VAE.
A novel dynamic nested Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) strategy is employed during training to induce a provable PCA-like, coarse-to-fine hierarchical structure in the concept tokens.

The diffusion-based decoder inherently reconstructs images from low to high frequencies, offloading spectral details and encouraging concept tokens to primarily encode semantic information, thereby decoupling semantic and spectral content.
paper_summary.summary: A visual tokenization framework called SEMANTICIST develops a compact, interpretable 1D token sequence for images by integrating PCA-like structure and resolving semantic-spectrum coupling, yielding improved reconstruction FID scores and efficient generative modeling.

title: "Principal Components" Enable A New Language of Images

abstract:
We present Deep Compression Autoencoder (DC-AE), a new family of autoencoder
models for accelerating high-resolution diffusion models. Existing autoencoder
models have demonstrated impressive results at a moderate spatial compression
ratio (e.g., 8x), but fail to maintain satisfactory reconstruction accuracy for
high spatial compression ratios (e.g., 64x). We address this challenge by
introducing two key techniques: (1) Residual Autoencoding, where we design our
models to learn residuals based on the space-to-channel transformed features to
alleviate the optimization difficulty of high spatial-compression autoencoders;
(2) Decoupled High-Resolution Adaptation, an efficient decoupled three-phases
training strategy for mitigating the generalization penalty of high
spatial-compression autoencoders. With these designs, we improve the
autoencoder's spatial compression ratio up to 128 while maintaining the
reconstruction quality. Applying our DC-AE to latent diffusion models, we
achieve significant speedup without accuracy drop. For example, on ImageNet
512x512, our DC-AE provides 19.1x inference speedup and 17.9x training speedup
on H100 GPU for UViT-H while achieving a better FID, compared with the widely
used SD-VAE-f8 autoencoder. Our code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: High spatial-compression autoencoders face optimization difficulties and a generalization penalty across resolutions, leading to severe reconstruction quality degradation.
Shifting spatial compression from the diffusion model's patch embedding to a specialized autoencoder (DC-AE) improves overall image generation quality and efficiency.

Decoupling the training process into low-resolution foundation, high-resolution adaptation, and low-resolution local refinement phases is effective for high-resolution autoencoder training without high costs.
originalProblem: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) operating with standard 8x spatial compression (f8) are computationally expensive for high-resolution image synthesis (e.g., 1024x1024+), especially for diffusion transformer models.
Increasing the spatial compression ratio of existing autoencoders (e.g., to 64x or 128x) leads to a drastic degradation in image reconstruction accuracy, rendering the latent space unusable.

Existing autoencoders struggle to generalize from low-resolution training to high-resolution inference when designed for high spatial compression, and full high-resolution training is costly and unstable.
results: DC-AE achieves superior image reconstruction accuracy, with rFID improvements from 16.84 (SD-VAE) to 0.22 for ImageNet 512x512 with f64c128 compression.

Integration of DC-AE provides substantial efficiency gains for high-resolution diffusion models, yielding up to 17.9x higher training throughput and 19.1x higher inference throughput for UViT-H on ImageNet 512x512.
DC-AE improves image generation quality, leading to better FID scores (e.g., UViT-H with DC-AE-f64 improves ImageNet 512x512 FID from 3.55 to 3.01) while handling higher spatial compression.
solution: Introduces Deep Compression Autoencoder (DC-AE) with "Residual Autoencoding" where network modules learn residuals from non-parametric space-to-channel and channel-to-space operations to ease optimization difficulty.

Proposes "Decoupled High-Resolution Adaptation," a three-phase training strategy: low-resolution full training, high-resolution latent adaptation (tuning only middle layers), and low-resolution local refinement with GAN loss (tuning only decoder head layers).
Integrates DC-AE into latent diffusion model pipelines by setting the diffusion transformer's patch size to 1, offloading the majority of spatial compression to the autoencoder.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MIT and NVIDIA developed the Deep Compression Autoencoder (DC-AE), enabling latent diffusion models to achieve spatial compression ratios up to 128x while preserving image quality. This approach yields up to 19.1x faster inference and 17.9x faster training for high-resolution image generation, improving image generation FID scores by allowing the diffusion model to operate on a significantly smaller latent space.

title: Deep Compression Autoencoder for Efficient High-Resolution Diffusion  Models

abstract: We present DC-AE 1.5, a new family of deep compression autoencoders for high-resolution diffusion models. Increasing the autoencoder's latent channel number is a highly effective approach for improving its reconstruction quality. However, it results in slow convergence for diffusion models, leading to poorer generation quality despite better reconstruction quality. This issue limits the quality upper bound of latent diffusion models and hinders the employment of autoencoders with higher spatial compression ratios. We introduce two key innovations to address this challenge: i) Structured Latent Space, a training-based approach to impose a desired channel-wise structure on the latent space with front latent channels capturing object structures and latter latent channels capturing image details; ii) Augmented Diffusion Training, an augmented diffusion training strategy with additional diffusion training objectives on object latent channels to accelerate convergence. With these techniques, DC-AE 1.5 delivers faster convergence and better diffusion scaling results than DC-AE. On ImageNet 512x512, DC-AE-1.5-f64c128 delivers better image generation quality than DC-AE-f32c32 while being 4x faster. Code: this https URL.

keyInsights: The poor performance of diffusion models with high-channel autoencoders is not due to channel count itself, but how structural information is sparsely distributed across these channels.
Explicitly structuring the latent space during autoencoder training can make the representation more interpretable and efficient for subsequent diffusion model learning.
Leveraging this structured latent space with a corresponding augmented diffusion training strategy creates synergistic improvements in both convergence speed and final image quality.
originalProblem: A paradox exists where increasing autoencoder latent channels improves reconstruction quality but leads to slower diffusion model convergence and worse final generation quality (gFID).
High latent channel numbers cause a "sparsity issue" where crucial object structure information becomes diluted across channels, hindering diffusion models from efficiently learning coherence.
This problem limits the potential of efficient autoencoders with high spatial compression ratios (e.g., f64) for high-fidelity image generation, as they inherently require more latent channels.
results: DC-AE 1.5 achieves up to 6x faster diffusion model convergence and superior generation quality (e.g., gFID reduced from 17.38 to 10.82 for UViT-H on ImageNet 256x256) compared to its predecessor.

The framework enables a 4 times higher training throughput (450 images/s vs. 112 images/s) while also yielding better image generation quality (gFID 2.18 vs. 2.90) using highly efficient f64c128 autoencoders.
Ablation studies confirm that both the Structured Latent Space and Augmented Diffusion Training components are indispensable, with their combined effect leading to the performance gains.
solution: A Structured Latent Space (SLS) is created by modifying autoencoder training: a random channel mask is applied to latent features during decoding, forcing early channels to prioritize object structure.

Augmented Diffusion Training (ADT) applies the same channel-wise mask to both noisy latent features and target noise during diffusion model training, providing targeted signals for learning object structures.
This two-pronged approach ensures a "diffusion-friendly" latent space, allowing the diffusion model to learn structural information more efficiently and accelerate overall convergence.
paper_summary.summary: DC-AE 1.5, developed by NVIDIA researchers, improves Latent Diffusion Model performance by addressing slow convergence issues when using autoencoders with numerous latent channels. It introduces methods to structure the latent space and augment diffusion training, resulting in faster convergence, enhanced image generation quality, and a four-fold increase in training throughput for high-resolution image synthesis.

title: DC-AE 1.5: Accelerating Diffusion Model Convergence with Structured Latent Space

abstract: Visual tokenization via auto-encoding empowers state-of-the-art image and video generative models by compressing pixels into a latent space. Although scaling Transformer-based generators has been central to recent advances, the tokenizer component itself is rarely scaled, leaving open questions about how auto-encoder design choices influence both its objective of reconstruction and downstream generative performance. Our work aims to conduct an exploration of scaling in auto-encoders to fill in this blank. To facilitate this exploration, we replace the typical convolutional backbone with an enhanced Vision Transformer architecture for Tokenization (ViTok). We train ViTok on large-scale image and video datasets far exceeding ImageNet-1K, removing data constraints on tokenizer scaling. We first study how scaling the auto-encoder bottleneck affects both reconstruction and generation -- and find that while it is highly correlated with reconstruction, its relationship with generation is more complex. We next explored the effect of separately scaling the auto-encoders' encoder and decoder on reconstruction and generation performance. Crucially, we find that scaling the encoder yields minimal gains for either reconstruction or generation, while scaling the decoder boosts reconstruction but the benefits for generation are mixed. Building on our exploration, we design ViTok as a lightweight auto-encoder that achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art auto-encoders on ImageNet-1K and COCO reconstruction tasks (256p and 512p) while outperforming existing auto-encoders on 16-frame 128p video reconstruction for UCF-101, all with 2-5x fewer FLOPs. When integrated with Diffusion Transformers, ViTok demonstrates competitive performance on image generation for ImageNet-1K and sets new state-of-the-art benchmarks for class-conditional video generation on UCF-101.

keyInsights: Total number of floating points in latent code (E) is the key bottleneck for reconstruction quality
Scaling the encoder provides no benefits and can hurt generation performance
Scaling the decoder improves reconstruction but has mixed effects on generation
Videos exhibit similar bottleneck characteristics as images but are more compressible
There's a fundamental trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and generation quality
originalProblem: Traditional visual tokenizers use convolutional neural networks which are difficult to scale effectively
Prior work has focused on scaling generators but not tokenizers, leaving open questions about optimal tokenizer design
Data constraints have limited exploration of tokenizer scaling beyond ImageNet-1K
results: ViTok matches or exceeds SOTA performance while using 2-5x fewer FLOPs
Achieves competitive image reconstruction and generation on ImageNet-1K and COCO
Sets new SOTA for class-conditional video generation on UCF-101
Shows videos can achieve similar quality with 4-8x less latent information than images
Demonstrates that scaling tokenizers alone does not consistently improve generation
solution: Introduced ViTok, a Vision Transformer-based tokenizer architecture for both images and videos
Trained models on large-scale datasets beyond ImageNet to remove data constraints
Systematically explored scaling of bottleneck size, encoder size, and decoder size
Evaluated impact on both reconstruction and generation tasks
paper_summary.summary: This paper explores how scaling affects visual tokenizer performance in image and video processing tasks.

title: Learnings from Scaling Visual Tokenizers for Reconstruction and Generation

abstract: In this work, we propose aligning pretrained visual encoders to serve as tokenizers for latent diffusion models in image generation. Unlike training a variational autoencoder (VAE) from scratch, which primarily emphasizes low-level details, our approach leverages the rich semantic structure of foundation encoders. We introduce a three-stage alignment strategy called AlignTok: (1) freeze the encoder and train an adapter and a decoder to establish a semantic latent space; (2) jointly optimize all components with an additional semantic preservation loss, enabling the encoder to capture perceptual details while retaining high-level semantics; and (3) refine the decoder for improved reconstruction quality. This alignment yields semantically rich image tokenizers that benefit diffusion models. On ImageNet 256$\times$256, our tokenizer accelerates the convergence of diffusion models, reaching a gFID of 1.90 within just 64 epochs, and improves generation both with and without classifier-free guidance. Scaling to LAION, text-to-image models trained with our tokenizer consistently outperforms FLUX VAE and VA-VAE under the same training steps. Overall, our method is simple, scalable, and establishes a semantically grounded paradigm for continuous tokenizer design.
title: AlignTok: Aligning Visual Foundation Encoders to Tokenizers for Diffusion Models

abstract: Unifying multimodal understanding, generation and reconstruction representation in a single tokenizer remains a key challenge in building unified models. Previous research predominantly attempts to address this in a dual encoder paradigm, e.g., utilizing the separate encoders for understanding and generation respectively or balancing semantic representations and low-level features with contrastive loss. In this paper, we propose VQRAE, a Vector Quantization version of Representation AutoEncoders, which pioneers the first exploration in unified representation to produce Continuous semantic features for image understanding and Discrete tokens for visual generation within a unified tokenizer. Specifically, we build upon pretrained vision foundation models with a symmetric ViT decoder and adopt a two-stage training strategy: first, it freezes the encoder and learns a high-dimensional semantic VQ codebook with pixel reconstruction objective; then jointly optimizes the encoder with self-distillation constraints. This design enables negligible semantic information for maintaining the ability of multimodal understanding, discrete tokens that are compatible for generation and fine-grained reconstruction. Besides, we identify the intriguing property in quantizing semantic encoders that rely on high-dimensional codebook in contrast to the previous common practice of low-dimensional codebook in image reconstruction. The semantic VQ codebook can achieve a 100% utilization ratio at a dimension of 1536. VQRAE presents competitive performance on several benchmarks of visual understanding, generation and reconstruction with promising scaling property in the autoregressive paradigm for its discrete merits.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology (HUST)

keyInsights: Quantizing semantic features from ViT-based VFMs requires a high-dimensional VQ codebook (e.g., 1536 dimensions) to avoid collapse and achieve 100% utilization, a departure from traditional low-dimensional codebooks for pixel-level features.
A single, convolution-free tokenizer built on VFMs can simultaneously provide discriminative continuous features for semantic clustering and discrete tokens for fine-grained texture clustering.

Self-distillation using a frozen VFM teacher is crucial during the tokenizer's fine-tuning phase to effectively preserve the original semantic understanding capabilities of the VFM while refining reconstruction quality.
originalProblem: Existing visual tokenizers face a fundamental conflict between providing continuous, high-level features for semantic understanding and discrete, fine-grained tokens for generation and reconstruction.
Dual-encoder architectures, which use separate encoders for understanding and generation, increase model complexity and training overhead, hindering deeper interaction between representations.
Prior discrete tokenizers, often optimized for pixel-level reconstruction, typically suffer from semantic information loss and degraded performance in high-level multimodal understanding tasks.
results: VQRAE achieves competitive image reconstruction quality (e.g., rFID of 1.39, PSNR of 22.88 on ImageNet-1K) without using any convolutional blocks in its tokenizer.

The model consistently outperforms other unified tokenizers across a suite of multimodal understanding benchmarks (e.g., POPE, GQA, MMBench) when integrated into MLLMs, without requiring specific multimodal alignment tuning.
VQRAE enables competitive visual generation with lightweight autoregressive models (0.6B parameters), achieving an overall GenEval score of 0.76 and a DPG-Bench score of 86.67.
solution: VQRAE uses a single, pre-trained Vision Foundation Model (VFM) as a unified encoder to produce continuous semantic features for understanding.
A high-dimensional Vector Quantization (VQ) codebook discretizes these semantic features, enabling fine-grained reconstruction and efficient autoregressive generation.

A two-stage training strategy, including self-distillation from a frozen VFM teacher, balances the preservation of semantic understanding with the acquisition of high-fidelity reconstruction capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: VQRAE introduces a unified visual tokenizer that concurrently generates continuous semantic features for understanding and discrete tokens for generation and reconstruction, leveraging pre-trained Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) as its encoder. This approach achieves competitive performance in image reconstruction and multimodal understanding benchmarks while enabling effective visual generation with lightweight models.

title: VQRAE: Representation Quantization Autoencoders for Multimodal Understanding, Generation and Reconstruction

abstract:
Efficient image tokenization with high compression ratios remains a critical
challenge for training generative models. We present SoftVQ-VAE, a continuous
image tokenizer that leverages soft categorical posteriors to aggregate
multiple codewords into each latent token, substantially increasing the
representation capacity of the latent space. When applied to Transformer-based
architectures, our approach compresses 256x256 and 512x512 images using as few
as 32 or 64 1-dimensional tokens. Not only does SoftVQ-VAE show consistent and
high-quality reconstruction, more importantly, it also achieves
state-of-the-art and significantly faster image generation results across
different denoising-based generative models. Remarkably, SoftVQ-VAE improves
inference throughput by up to 18x for generating 256x256 images and 55x for
512x512 images while achieving competitive FID scores of 1.78 and 2.21 for
SiT-XL. It also improves the training efficiency of the generative models by
reducing the number of training iterations by 2.3x while maintaining comparable
performance. With its fully-differentiable design and semantic-rich latent
space, our experiment demonstrates that SoftVQ-VAE achieves efficient
tokenization without compromising generation quality, paving the way for more
efficient generative models. Code and model are released.

keyInsights: Soft (continuous) quantization, enabled by a soft categorical posterior, allows for significantly higher image compression ratios while preserving reconstruction quality compared to traditional discrete quantization methods.

The fully differentiable nature of SoftVQ-VAE's latent space enables direct regularization and alignment with features from powerful pre-trained vision encoders, leading to more semantically rich and discriminative tokens.

The quality and semantic richness of the learned latent space, rather than just reconstruction fidelity, correlate strongly with the performance of downstream generative models, leading to better generation quality and efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing image tokenizers (KL-VAE, VQ-VAE) produce hundreds to thousands of tokens, creating a computational bottleneck due to the quadratic complexity of Transformer-based generative models.
Achieving very high compression ratios with current tokenizers typically leads to a substantial degradation in image reconstruction and generation quality.

It is challenging for existing tokenizers to learn discriminative and semantically meaningful latent representations, especially when pushed to high compression ratios, limiting the quality of generated images.
results: SoftVQ-VAE achieves high-quality image reconstruction with extreme compression, yielding rFID scores of 0.61-0.89 for 256x256 images using as few as 32-64 tokens, outperforming existing tokenizers with fewer tokens.

When integrated with generative models like SiT and DiT, SoftVQ-VAE enables state-of-the-art image generation performance (e.g., FID 1.78 on ImageNet 256x256 with 64 tokens) while using drastically fewer tokens.

It delivers substantial efficiency gains, including up to 55x higher inference throughput for 512x512 images and significantly reduced training time and GFLOPs for generative models compared to KL-tokenized counterparts.
solution: SoftVQ-VAE uses a Vision Transformer-based architecture with learnable 1D tokens and a novel 'soft categorical posterior' to continuously aggregate information from a codebook, making the entire tokenization process fully differentiable.

It eliminates the need for the 'straight-through' trick and auxiliary losses (codebook, commit loss) common in VQ-VAE, simplifying optimization and enabling direct gradient flow for the codebook and encoder.

The model incorporates a representation alignment strategy, initializing its encoder with DINOv2 weights and aligning its latent codes with features from a pre-trained DINOv2 vision encoder to learn semantically rich representations.
paper_summary.summary: SoftVQ-VAE, developed by researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and AMD, introduces an efficient 1-dimensional continuous tokenizer that utilizes a soft categorical posterior for extreme image compression. This method achieves high-quality reconstruction with as few as 32-64 tokens for high-resolution images, leading to up to 55x higher inference throughput and reduced training costs for Transformer-based generative models while enabling competitive image generation.

title: SoftVQ-VAE: Efficient 1-Dimensional Continuous Tokenizer

abstract: We introduce Representation Tokenizer (RepTok), a generative modeling framework that represents an image using a single continuous latent token obtained from self-supervised vision transformers. Building on a pre-trained SSL encoder, we fine-tune only the semantic token embedding and pair it with a generative decoder trained jointly using a standard flow matching objective. This adaptation enriches the token with low-level, reconstruction-relevant details, enabling faithful image reconstruction. To preserve the favorable geometry of the original SSL space, we add a cosine-similarity loss that regularizes the adapted token, ensuring the latent space remains smooth and suitable for generation. Our single-token formulation resolves spatial redundancies of 2D latent spaces and significantly reduces training costs. Despite its simplicity and efficiency, RepTok achieves competitive results on class-conditional ImageNet generation and naturally extends to text-to-image synthesis, reaching competitive zero-shot performance on MS-COCO under extremely limited training budgets. Our findings highlight the potential of fine-tuned SSL representations as compact and effective latent spaces for efficient generative modeling.

keyInsights: A single continuous token derived from an adapted SSL model can serve as an exceptionally compact, semantically rich, and geometrically stable latent space, enabling high-fidelity image reconstruction.

Minimal fine-tuning of only the `[cls]` token, coupled with a cosine-similarity regularization loss, effectively infuses reconstruction-relevant visual details into the semantic token without compromising the pre-trained SSL space's smooth geometry.

Leveraging the inherent compactness of the single-token latent space allows for the use of efficient, attention-free MLP-Mixer architectures for generation, drastically reducing computational demands.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art generative models, including latent diffusion models, suffer from high computational demands due to operations on high-dimensional pixel or 2D grid-based latent spaces.
Traditional 2D latent spaces, while reducing dimensionality from pixel space, still retain significant spatial redundancies inherent to natural images.
The full potential of powerful pre-trained Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) representations as direct, efficient, and semantically rich latent spaces for generative modeling has been underexplored.
results: RepTok achieves competitive FID scores on ImageNet 256x256 class-conditional generation (e.g., 3.22 FID) while utilizing over 90% less training FLOPs compared to leading transformer-based diffusion baselines.

It demonstrates high-fidelity single-token reconstructions on ImageNet (rFID 1.85, PSNR 14.94, SSIM 33.26), indicating the adapted token successfully captures both high-level semantics and low-level details.

The framework scales to text-to-image synthesis, achieving competitive zero-shot FID performance on MS-COCO with an extremely limited training budget (e.g., <20 hours on four A100 GPUs for 200k iterations).
solution: RepTok adapts a pre-trained SSL vision transformer encoder to produce a single continuous latent token, fine-tuning only the `[cls]` token embedding to inject reconstruction details while freezing the rest of the encoder.

A generative decoder is jointly trained with the adapted encoder using a flow matching objective and a cosine-similarity loss, ensuring faithful image reconstruction and preserving the SSL space's beneficial geometry.
For latent space generation, a lightweight, attention-free MLP-Mixer architecture is employed, further reducing computational overhead, and extended with cross-attention for text-to-image synthesis.
paper_summary.summary: RepTok introduces a framework that adapts pooled 1D output tokens from pre-trained self-supervised vision transformers as an exceptionally compact, continuous latent space for image generation. This approach achieves competitive ImageNet generation quality with over 90% less training computation compared to existing transformer-based diffusion models and demonstrates strong performance in text-to-image synthesis under limited budgets.

title: Adapting Self-Supervised Representations as a Latent Space for Efficient Generation

abstract: Recent work leverages Vision Foundation Models as image encoders to boost the generative performance of latent diffusion models (LDMs), as their semantic feature distributions are easy to learn. However, such semantic features often lack low-level information (\eg, color and texture), leading to degraded reconstruction fidelity, which has emerged as a primary bottleneck in further scaling LDMs. To address this limitation, we propose LV-RAE, a representation autoencoder that augments semantic features with missing low-level information, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction while remaining highly aligned with the semantic distribution. We further observe that the resulting high-dimensional, information-rich latent make decoders sensitive to latent perturbations, causing severe artifacts when decoding generated latent and consequently degrading generation quality. Our analysis suggests that this sensitivity primarily stems from excessive decoder responses along directions off the data manifold. Building on these insights, we propose fine-tuning the decoder to increase its robustness and smoothing the generated latent via controlled noise injection, thereby enhancing generation quality. Experiments demonstrate that LV-RAE significantly improves reconstruction fidelity while preserving the semantic abstraction and achieving strong generative quality. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Treating VFM semantic features as a fixed base manifold and separately learning local variations is more effective than directly fine-tuning the VFM for reconstruction, ensuring stable training and preserving semantic alignment.
Augmenting VFM features with learned low-level details allows for high-fidelity reconstruction without sacrificing the semantic richness beneficial for diffusion models.

High-dimensional latent spaces can make decoders overly sensitive to off-manifold perturbations; introducing controlled noise during decoder training and inference significantly improves generative stability and quality.
originalProblem: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) integrating Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) as encoders suffer from a reconstruction bottleneck, lacking low-level visual details in generated images.
VFMs excel at high-level semantic features but inherently miss fine-grained information, leading to degraded visual fidelity in reconstructed images.
Directly fine-tuning VFMs for reconstruction causes competing objectives, destabilizing training and preventing consistent convergence for both semantic alignment and low-level details.
results: LV-RAE achieved state-of-the-art reconstruction performance with a PSNR of 32.50, SSIM of 0.938, and LPIPS of 0.017 on ImageNet-1K, outperforming baselines like SD-VAE and SVG.
Demonstrated strong class-conditional image generation, achieving a gFID of 2.42 and an Inception Score (IS) of 223.84 on ImageNet 256x256 using a DiT_DH-XL backbone.

The decoder fine-tuning strategy with noise augmentation and inference-time noise injection significantly enhanced decoder robustness, leading to improved generative quality (lower FID and FDD) by smoothing out off-manifold artifacts.
solution: Introduced LV-RAE (Local-Variations Augmented Representation Autoencoder), which uses a frozen VFM for semantic features and a separate lightweight encoder to learn and add missing low-level information.
Formed a complete latent by element-wise adding learned low-level variations to VFM features, regularized by an alignment loss to maintain semantic consistency.

Developed a two-stage decoder training strategy with noise augmentation during fine-tuning, and controlled noise injection during inference, to enhance decoder robustness to latent perturbations and mitigate visual artifacts.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nankai University and Tencent Inc. developed LV-RAE, a Local-Variations Augmented Representation Autoencoder, to overcome the reconstruction bottleneck in latent diffusion models using Vision Foundation Models. This approach augments semantic features with crucial low-level details and enhances decoder robustness, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction fidelity with a PSNR of 32.50 on ImageNet-1K and competitive generative performance, including a gFID of 2.42 on ImageNet 256x256.

title: Improving Reconstruction of Representation Autoencoder

abstract:
Image tokenization has significantly advanced visual generation and
multimodal modeling, particularly when paired with autoregressive models.
However, current methods face challenges in balancing efficiency and fidelity:
high-resolution image reconstruction either requires an excessive number of
tokens or compromises critical details through token reduction. To resolve
this, we propose Latent Consistency Tokenizer (Layton) that bridges discrete
visual tokens with the compact latent space of pre-trained Latent Diffusion
Models (LDMs), enabling efficient representation of 1024x1024 images using only
256 tokens-a 16 times compression over VQGAN. Layton integrates a transformer
encoder, a quantized codebook, and a latent consistency decoder. Direct
application of LDM as the decoder results in color and brightness
discrepancies. Thus, we convert it to latent consistency decoder, reducing
multi-step sampling to 1-2 steps for direct pixel-level supervision.
Experiments demonstrate Layton's superiority in high-fidelity reconstruction,
with 10.8 reconstruction Frechet Inception Distance on MSCOCO-2017 5K benchmark
for 1024x1024 image reconstruction. We also extend Layton to a text-to-image
generation model, LaytonGen, working in autoregression. It achieves 0.73 score
on GenEval benchmark, surpassing current state-of-the-art methods. Project
homepage: this https URL
title: Layton: Latent Consistency Tokenizer for 1024-pixel Image Reconstruction  and Generation by 256 Tokens

abstract: Recent video generation models largely rely on video autoencoders that compress pixel-space videos into latent representations. However, existing video autoencoders suffer from three major limitations: (1) fixed-rate compression that wastes tokens on simple videos, (2) inflexible CNN architectures that prevent variable-length latent modeling, and (3) deterministic decoders that struggle to recover appropriate details from compressed latents. To address these issues, we propose One-Dimensional Diffusion Video Autoencoder (One-DVA), a transformer-based framework for adaptive 1D encoding and diffusion-based decoding. The encoder employs query-based vision transformers to extract spatiotemporal features and produce latent representations, while a variable-length dropout mechanism dynamically adjusts the latent length. The decoder is a pixel-space diffusion transformer that reconstructs videos with the latents as input conditions. With a two-stage training strategy, One-DVA achieves performance comparable to 3D-CNN VAEs on reconstruction metrics at identical compression ratios. More importantly, it supports adaptive compression and thus can achieve higher compression ratios. To better support downstream latent generation, we further regularize the One-DVA latent distribution for generative modeling and fine-tune its decoder to mitigate artifacts caused by the generation process.

keyInsights: Adaptive, motion-aware variable-length 1D latent encoding improves token efficiency and reconstruction quality compared to fixed-length compression.
Treating video reconstruction as a conditional generative task with a diffusion decoder enhances detail recovery, especially when latents are short or insufficient.
A two-stage training procedure (deterministic pretraining followed by stochastic post-training) is crucial for robust feature learning and optimizing the autoencoder's performance.
Latent space alignment and fine-tuning the decoder with LDM-sampled latents effectively mitigate visual artifacts in downstream generative models.
originalProblem: Existing video autoencoders use a fixed number of latent tokens, leading to inefficient resource allocation regardless of video complexity.
Many current video autoencoders rely on rigid 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), which are inflexible for adaptive compression or variable-shaped inputs/outputs.
Deterministic decoders in lossy compression struggle to recover missing pixel-level details, resulting in blurry outputs or reconstruction artifacts.
results: One-DVA achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction, with a PSNR of 36.48 and SSIM of 0.95, and competitive rFVD (56.96), often surpassing 3D-CNN VAEs.
Adaptive compression using 55.8% of 1D tokens improves reconstruction (rFVD 70.28) compared to fixed-length encoding with the same average token count (rFVD 72.42).
The framework supports high-quality text-to-video generation, achieving a gFVD of 210.9, comparable to other advanced methods, and significantly reduces generation artifacts.
solution: A Query-based Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder generates compact 1D latent sequences and a structural latent, leveraging transformer flexibility.
A novel motion-aware, variable-length latent-dropout module adaptively compresses 1D latents during training, optimizing token efficiency based on video complexity.
A pixel-space Diffusion Transformer (DiT) acts as a generative decoder, inferring missing details from compressed latents and noisy inputs to reconstruct high-fidelity videos.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed One-DVA, an adaptive 1D video diffusion autoencoder that dynamically adjusts compression based on video content and utilizes a generative diffusion decoder for enhanced reconstruction. The framework provides a flexible latent space for video generation, achieving competitive performance in reconstruction metrics and supporting high-quality text-to-video tasks.

title: Adaptive 1D Video Diffusion Autoencoder

abstract: Current image generation methods are based on a two-stage training approach. In stage 1, an auto-encoder is trained to compress an image into a latent space; in stage 2, a generative model is trained to learn a distribution over that latent space. This reveals a fundamental trade-off, do we compress more aggressively to make the latent distribution easier for the stage 2 model to learn even if it makes reconstruction worse? We study this problem in the context of discrete, auto-regressive image generation. Through the lens of scaling laws, we show that smaller stage 2 models can benefit from more compressed stage 1 latents even if reconstruction performance worsens, demonstrating that generation modeling capacity plays a role in this trade-off. Diving deeper, we rigorously study the connection between compute scaling and the stage 1 rate-distortion trade-off. Next, we introduce Causally Regularized Tokenization (CRT), which uses knowledge of the stage 2 generation modeling procedure to embed useful inductive biases in stage 1 latents. This regularization improves stage 2 generation performance better by making the tokens easier to model without affecting the stage 1 compression rate and marginally affecting distortion: we are able to improve compute efficiency 2-3$\times$ over baseline. Finally, we use CRT with further optimizations to the visual tokenizer setup to result in a generative pipeline that matches LlamaGen-3B generation performance (2.18 FID) with half the tokens per image (256 vs. 576) and a fourth the total model parameters (775M vs. 3.1B) while using the same architecture and inference procedure.
title: When Worse is Better: Navigating the compression-generation tradeoff in visual tokenization

abstract: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) aims to compress pixel data into low-dimensional latent space, playing an important role in OpenAI's Sora and other latent video diffusion generation models. While most of existing video VAEs inflate a pretrained image VAE into the 3D causal structure for temporal-spatial compression, this paper presents two astonishing findings: (1) The initialization from a well-trained image VAE with the same latent dimensions suppresses the improvement of subsequent temporal compression capabilities. (2) The adoption of causal reasoning leads to unequal information interactions and unbalanced performance between frames. To alleviate these problems, we propose a keyframe-based temporal compression (KTC) architecture and a group causal convolution (GCConv) module to further improve video VAE (IV-VAE). Specifically, the KTC architecture divides the latent space into two branches, in which one half completely inherits the compression prior of keyframes from a lower-dimension image VAE while the other half involves temporal compression through 3D group causal convolution, reducing temporal-spatial conflicts and accelerating the convergence speed of video VAE. The GCConv in above 3D half uses standard convolution within each frame group to ensure inter-frame equivalence, and employs causal logical padding between groups to maintain flexibility in processing variable frame video. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate the SOTA video reconstruction and generation capabilities of the proposed IV-VAE (this https URL).

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Initializing a video VAE with the same latent channel dimensions from a pre-trained image VAE actually hinders temporal compression learning, suggesting a need for a more balanced spatial-temporal prior integration.

The unidirectional information flow of standard 3D causal convolution within frame groups causes inter-frame performance imbalance and flickering, which can be mitigated by allowing bidirectional interaction within groups while preserving causality across groups.

Expanding the model's receptive field through methods like atrous convolutions and strategically placed attention modules is crucial for effectively perceiving and encoding complex motion patterns, especially in high-resolution video.
originalProblem: Common practice of initializing 3D video VAEs from 2D image VAEs with the same latent channel dimensions suppresses the improvement of temporal compression capabilities.
Standard 3D causal convolution leads to unequal information interactions and inter-frame performance imbalance (flickering) within a group of frames.
Existing open-source video datasets for evaluating VAEs lack sufficient resolution or diverse motion, failing to reflect real-world video challenges.
results: IV-VAE achieved state-of-the-art reconstruction performance, including an 58% reduction in FVD on Kinetics-600 with 73% fewer parameters compared to OpenSora VAE, and superior FVD scores for video generation with the Latte diffusion model.

Qualitative analyses and SSIM values demonstrated that IV-VAE significantly reduced flickering and achieved more balanced frame performance and superior temporal consistency compared to other methods.

The model exhibited robust performance across 480P, 720P, and 1080P resolutions on the new MotionHD dataset, with performance gains widening at higher resolutions (e.g., PSNR improvement over CogX-VAE increased from 0.28 at 480P to 1.65 at 1080P).
solution: A Keyframe-based Temporal Compression (KTC) architecture with a dual-branch structure (2D for spatial keyframes, 3D for temporal motion) that leverages lower-dimension image VAE priors.
A Group Causal Convolution (GCConv) module that applies standard 3D convolution within frame groups for bidirectional information flow, while maintaining causal logical padding between groups.
Temporal Motion Perception Enhancement (TMPE) using parallel atrous convolutions and expanded attention modules to enhance the model's receptive field for high-resolution motion capture.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from USTC and Tongyi Lab developed an Improved Video VAE (IV-VAE) that optimizes latent video diffusion models by refining image VAE initialization and addressing inter-frame inconsistencies in causal convolutions. This approach achieves state-of-the-art video reconstruction and generation, demonstrating superior temporal consistency and performance across varied resolutions, while also introducing the MotionHD dataset for robust evaluation.

title: Improved Video VAE for Latent Video Diffusion Model

abstract:
In generative modeling, tokenization simplifies complex data into compact,
structured representations, creating a more efficient, learnable space. For
high-dimensional visual data, it reduces redundancy and emphasizes key features
for high-quality generation. Current visual tokenization methods rely on a
traditional autoencoder framework, where the encoder compresses data into
latent representations, and the decoder reconstructs the original input. In
this work, we offer a new perspective by proposing denoising as decoding,
shifting from single-step reconstruction to iterative refinement. Specifically,
we replace the decoder with a diffusion process that iteratively refines noise
to recover the original image, guided by the latents provided by the encoder.
We evaluate our approach by assessing both reconstruction (rFID) and generation
quality (FID), comparing it to state-of-the-art autoencoding approaches. By
adopting iterative reconstruction through diffusion, our autoencoder, namely
Epsilon-VAE, achieves high reconstruction quality, which in turn enhances
downstream generation quality by 22% at the same compression rates or provides
2.3x inference speedup through increasing compression rates. We hope this work
offers new insights into integrating iterative generation and autoencoding for
improved compression and generation.

keyInsights: Reframing visual decoding as an iterative denoising process with a diffusion model significantly improves both reconstruction quality and the effectiveness of latent representations for downstream generative tasks.

Integrating specific architectural choices (UNet for decoder), training objectives (rectified flow, time-dependent adversarial loss), and sampling schedules (logit-normal training, reversed logarithmic inference) is crucial for a performant diffusion-based autoencoder.
The ϵ-VAE approach allows for much higher compression rates without proportional quality loss, leading to substantial gains in inference efficiency and throughput for latent generative models.
originalProblem: Traditional autoencoders rely on a single-step, deterministic decoder, limiting their ability to perfectly reconstruct fine details, especially under high compression ratios.
The quality of latent representations generated by conventional VAEs can constrain the performance and efficiency of subsequent latent generative models, like latent diffusion models.
Achieving high compression rates in existing visual tokenizers often leads to a significant degradation in reconstruction quality, impeding computational efficiency and scalability.
results: ϵ-VAE achieves up to a 40% improvement in reconstruction quality (rFID) compared to standard VAEs, maintaining robust performance even at high compression ratios and generalizing to higher resolutions.

When integrated into latent diffusion models (e.g., DiT-XL/2), ϵ-VAE yields superior class-conditional image generation quality (lower FID) and enables 2.3x to 3.2x higher inference throughput due to enhanced compression.

Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of individual components, showing that rectified flow, logit-normal time sampling, and adversarial denoising trajectory matching are critical for the model's performance.
solution: The deterministic decoder of a traditional autoencoder is replaced with a stochastic, iterative diffusion model, framing visual decoding as a conditional denoising task.

A UNet-based diffusion decoder is trained using a rectified flow parameterization to predict velocity, iteratively refining noise to reconstruct the original image conditioned on encoder-provided latents.

The training objective combines a score-matching loss with perceptual matching (LPIPS) on estimated clean images and an adversarial loss enforcing denoising trajectory consistency using a time-dependent discriminator.
paper_summary.summary: Google DeepMind's ϵ-VAE redefines visual decoding by integrating an iterative denoising diffusion process within an autoencoder, yielding up to a 40% improvement in reconstruction quality (rFID) over standard VAEs. This approach enables latent diffusion models to achieve superior image generation quality (FID) while providing up to 3.2x faster inference throughput through higher compression.

title: Epsilon-VAE: Denoising as Visual Decoding

abstract: Diffusion models have become the dominant paradigm for image generation and editing, with latent diffusion models shifting denoising to a compact latent space for efficiency and scalability. Recent attempts to leverage pretrained visual representation models as tokenizer priors either align diffusion features to representation features or directly reuse representation encoders as frozen tokenizers. Although such approaches can improve generation metrics, they often suffer from limited reconstruction fidelity due to frozen encoders, which in turn degrades editing quality, as well as overly high-dimensional latents that make diffusion modeling difficult. To address these limitations, We propose Representation-Pivoted AutoEncoder, a representation-based tokenizer that improves both generation and editing. We introduce Representation-Pivot Regularization, a training strategy that enables a representation-initialized encoder to be fine-tuned for reconstruction while preserving the semantic structure of the pretrained representation space, followed by a variational bridge which compress latent space into a compact one for better diffusion modeling. We adopt an objective-decoupled stage-wise training strategy that sequentially optimizes generative tractability and reconstruction-fidelity objectives. Together, these components yield a tokenizer that preserves strong semantics, reconstructs faithfully, and produces latents with reduced diffusion modeling complexity. Experiments demonstrate that RPiAE outperforms other visual tokenizers on text-to-image generation and image editing, while delivering the best reconstruction fidelity among representation-based tokenizers.

keyInsights: It is possible to fine-tune a representation-initialized encoder for improved reconstruction fidelity without corrupting its inherited semantic structure, by employing a pivot regularization mechanism.

Decoupling the training objectives into sequential stages allows for effective optimization of competing requirements (semantic preservation, reconstruction, generative tractability) in the autoencoder's latent space.

Compressing high-dimensional representation features into a compact, lower-dimensional and KL-regularized latent space improves generative tractability for downstream diffusion models while retaining critical semantic information.
originalProblem: Latent Diffusion Models face a trade-off between reconstruction fidelity (crucial for editing) and generative tractability (for efficient diffusion training) in their latent spaces.

Existing internal-reused representation model (RM) methods achieve semantic structure but sacrifice reconstruction fidelity by freezing the RM encoder, while high dimensionality of raw RM features complicates diffusion modeling.
Traditional VAEs often produce latent spaces lacking well-structured geometry, making generative training challenging.
results: RPiAE achieved a gFID of 1.09 and Inception Score of 254.7 on ImageNet-1K, surpassing previous internal-RM models in class-conditional generation.

The system outperformed existing methods across text-to-image generation (GenEval, DPG-Bench) and image editing (GEdit-Bench-EN) benchmarks, demonstrating superior adherence to prompts and source identity preservation.

Ablation studies confirmed that the trainable encoder with Pivot Regularization improved reconstruction fidelity (rFID 0.50), and the multi-stage training strategy was crucial for overall performance.
solution: RPiAE introduces a trainable Representation-Model Encoder initialized from a pretrained visual RM (DINOv2-B), a Variational Bridge for latent compression, and a Decoder for pixel reconstruction.
A novel Pivot Regularization technique employs a Frozen Pivot Replica Encoder to supervise the trainable RM encoder, preventing semantic drift during fine-tuning.
