An objective-decoupled three-stage training strategy sequentially optimizes the encoder (for reconstruction and semantic stability), the Variational Bridge (for compact, regularized latents), and the decoder (for perceptual fidelity).
paper_summary.summary: RPiAE is a representation-pivoted autoencoder that enhances both image generation and editing within latent diffusion models by enabling reconstruction-oriented fine-tuning of a representation-initialized encoder while preserving its semantic structure. The system achieved state-of-the-art performance in class-conditional generation with a gFID of 1.09 on ImageNet-1K, and outperformed baselines in text-to-image synthesis and image editing benchmarks.

title: RPiAE: A Representation-Pivoted Autoencoder Enhancing Both Image Generation and Editing

abstract: The latent space of generative modeling is long dominated by the VAE encoder. The latents from the pretrained representation encoders (e.g., DINO, SigLIP, MAE) are previously considered inappropriate for generative modeling. Recently, RAE method lights the hope and reveals that the representation autoencoder can also achieve competitive performance as the VAE encoder. However, the integration of representation autoencoder into continuous autoregressive (AR) models, remains largely unexplored. In this work, we investigate the challenges of employing high-dimensional representation autoencoders within the AR paradigm, denoted as \textit{RAE-AR}. We focus on the unique properties of AR models and identify two primary hurdles: complex token-wise distribution modeling and the high-dimensionality amplified training-inference gap (exposure bias). To address these, we introduce token simplification via distribution normalization to ease modeling difficulty and improve convergence. Furthermore, we enhance prediction robustness by incorporating Gaussian noise injection during training to mitigate exposure bias. Our empirical results demonstrate that these modifications substantially bridge the performance gap, enabling representation autoencoder to achieve results comparable to traditional VAEs on AR models. This work paves the way for a more unified architecture across visual understanding and generative modeling.

keyInsights: High token variance in semantic representations poses a significant challenge for token-wise distribution modeling in continuous autoregressive systems.
Exposure bias in autoregressive generation is exacerbated by high-dimensional semantic latents, leading to substantial performance degradation without mitigation.
Token normalization eases the modeling complexity for AR models, while Gaussian noise injection addresses the training-inference gap caused by exposure bias.
originalProblem: Continuous autoregressive models primarily rely on reconstruction-based VAEs, neglecting the potential of rich, high-dimensional semantic encoders for generation.
Direct integration of high-dimensional semantic representation autoencoders into autoregressive models results in significantly degraded generative performance.
Challenges include modeling complex token-wise distributions from high-variance semantic tokens and an exacerbated exposure bias in sequential generation due to high-dimensionality.
results: Direct integration of semantic representation autoencoders into continuous autoregressive models resulted in significantly inferior generative performance, with SigLIP2 achieving a gFID of 34.084 compared to VAE's 3.237.

The RAE-AR framework, combining token normalization and Gaussian noise injection, enabled semantic RAEs to achieve generative performance comparable to or exceeding traditional VAEs; SigLIP2 RAE-AR reached a gFID of 6.091, outperforming VA-VAE's 5.983.
The proposed strategies for taming RAEs are effective in both causal raster-scan and mask-based autoregressive models, demonstrating broad applicability.
solution: Normalizing each semantic token to have zero mean and unit variance along its channel dimension during training, simplifying token-wise distribution modeling.
Injecting Gaussian noise into these normalized tokens during training, enhancing prediction robustness and mitigating exposure bias during inference.
Combining token normalization and Gaussian noise injection to create the RAE-AR framework.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed RAE-AR, a framework that integrates high-dimensional semantic representation autoencoders into continuous autoregressive models using token normalization and Gaussian noise injection. This approach allowed semantic RAEs to achieve generative performance comparable to or better than traditional VAEs, with SigLIP2 RAE-AR demonstrating a gFID of 6.091 compared to VA-VAE's 5.983.

title: RAE-AR: Taming Autoregressive Models with Representation Autoencoders

abstract: Reducing token count is crucial for efficient training and inference of latent diffusion models, especially at high resolution. A common strategy is to build high-compression image tokenizers with more channels per token. However, when trained only for reconstruction, high-dimensional latent spaces often lose meaningful structure, making diffusion training harder. Existing methods address this with extra objectives such as semantic alignment or selective dropout, but usually require costly diffusion retraining. Pretrained diffusion models, however, already exhibit a structured, lower-dimensional latent space; thus, a simpler idea is to expand the latent dimensionality while preserving this structure. We therefore propose \textbf{D}etail-\textbf{A}ligned VAE, which increases the compression ratio of a pretrained VAE with only lightweight adaptation of the pretrained diffusion backbone. DA-VAE uses an explicit latent layout: the first $C$ channels come directly from the pretrained VAE at a base resolution, while an additional $D$ channels encode higher-resolution details. A simple detail-alignment mechanism encourages the expanded latent space to retain the structure of the original one. With a warm-start fine-tuning strategy, our method enables $1024 \times 1024$ image generation with Stable Diffusion 3.5 using only $32 \times 32$ tokens, $4\times$ fewer than the original model, within 5 H100-days. It further unlocks $2048 \times 2048$ generation with SD3.5, achieving a $6\times$ speedup while preserving image quality. We also validate the method and its design choices quantitatively on ImageNet.

keyInsights: Aggressively increasing the channel dimension for detail encoding, rather than spatial resolution, allows for significant token count reduction while maintaining high effective resolution.
Enforcing explicit structure in the expanded latent space through a detail-alignment loss is crucial for stable diffusion training and for preventing unstructured residual encoding.

A carefully designed warm-start fine-tuning approach, leveraging zero-initialization and gradual loss scheduling, enables efficient adaptation of pretrained diffusion models, preserving their learned priors while integrating new detail information.
originalProblem: High computational cost for training and inference of high-resolution images in text-to-image diffusion models, stemming from the quadratic scaling of self-attention with latent tokens.
Existing high-compression Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) often create new latent spaces, necessitating costly retraining of the entire diffusion model from scratch.
Naively compressed latent spaces, when trained solely with reconstruction objectives, can lack meaningful structure, complicating or destabilizing downstream diffusion model training.
results: Achieved an FID-50k of 4.84 on 512x512 ImageNet generation, outperforming scratch-trained and fine-tuned LightningDiT-XL baselines (21.79 and 11.31 FID respectively) at the same token budget.
Enabled 1024x1024 text-to-image generation with a 4x speedup in throughput (1.03 img/s vs. 0.25 img/s for original SD3.5M) and competitive FID of 10.91, CLIP Score of 31.91, and GenEval of 0.64.

Demonstrated 2048x2048 image generation with a 6x speedup over the original SD3.5M, producing high-quality samples that maintain global structure and fine details, overcoming distortion issues faced by the original model.
solution: A structured latent space is introduced, where a new detail encoder expands the channel dimension to carry more high-resolution information, while the original base latent maintains global structure and spatial token count.
A latent alignment mechanism is employed, using a parameter-free grouped reduction loss, to encourage the detail latent to adopt a structure consistent with the pretrained base latent.

A warm-start fine-tuning strategy for pretrained Diffusion Transformers is used, incorporating zero-initialization of new components and gradual loss scheduling to efficiently adapt to the expanded latent space.
paper_summary.summary: DA-VAE introduces a plug-in latent compression technique for diffusion models that significantly reduces token count for high-resolution image generation by structuring the latent space and aligning fine-grained details. This approach enables existing pretrained Diffusion Transformers to generate images up to 2048x2048 with 4x to 6x speedups and improved quality through lightweight fine-tuning.

title: DA-VAE: Plug-in Latent Compression for Diffusion via Detail Alignment

abstract: Image tokenizers are crucial for visual generative models, e.g., diffusion models (DMs) and autoregressive (AR) models, as they construct the latent representation for modeling. Increasing token length is a common approach to improve the image reconstruction quality. However, tokenizers with longer token lengths are not guaranteed to achieve better generation quality. There exists a trade-off between reconstruction and generation quality regarding token length. In this paper, we investigate the impact of token length on both image reconstruction and generation and provide a flexible solution to the tradeoff. We propose ImageFolder, a semantic tokenizer that provides spatially aligned image tokens that can be folded during autoregressive modeling to improve both generation efficiency and quality. To enhance the representative capability without increasing token length, we leverage dual-branch product quantization to capture different contexts of images. Specifically, semantic regularization is introduced in one branch to encourage compacted semantic information while another branch is designed to capture the remaining pixel-level details. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior quality of image generation and shorter token length with ImageFolder tokenizer.

keyInsights: Decoupling the total information content from the effective sequence length can resolve the trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and AR generation efficiency/quality.
Product quantization with a dual-branch architecture allows for the efficient capture of distinct semantic and detail information, reducing inter-dependency between these token types.
Applying semantic regularization to a dedicated branch is more effective than universal semantic injection, preserving pixel-level detail while enhancing semantic representation.
originalProblem: AR image generation models face a trade-off: longer token sequences improve reconstruction but increase computational costs and error propagation, while shorter sequences degrade detail.
Existing semantic tokenizers achieve compression by sacrificing pixel-level details, leading to distorted image reconstructions.
The quadratic computational complexity of transformer-based AR models with respect to sequence length limits their scalability and integration with large language models.
results: Achieved a gFID of 2.60 on ImageNet 256x256 with an AR modeling token length of 286, outperforming LlamaGen-L (gFID 3.80) and VAR (gFID 3.30 with 680 tokens).
Demonstrated high image reconstruction quality with an rFID of 0.80 and achieved 100% codebook utilization.
Showed significantly higher linear probing accuracy (58.0) on ImageNet compared to LlamaGen-L (2.6) and VAR (11.3), indicating a more semantically rich latent space.
solution: ImageFolder utilizes a dual-branch product quantization scheme within its tokenizer, separating semantic and detail information for efficient representation.
Semantic regularization is applied to only one branch using a pre-trained DINOv2 model and CLIP-style contrastive loss to ensure compact semantic encoding without compromising pixel details.
An autoregressive model performs parallel prediction of both semantic and detail tokens from a single logit, effectively halving the sequence length presented to the generator.
paper_summary.summary: ImageFolder presents an autoregressive image generation tokenizer that combines high-fidelity image reconstruction with efficient generation by leveraging product quantization and parallel token prediction. The method yields competitive generation quality with a notably shorter effective sequence length, achieving a gFID of 2.60 with 286 tokens while maintaining an rFID of 0.80 on ImageNet 256x256.

title: ImageFolder: Autoregressive Image Generation with Folded Tokens

abstract: Recent advances in multimodal models highlight the pivotal role of image tokenization in high-resolution image generation. By compressing images into compact latent representations, tokenizers enable generative models to operate in lower-dimensional spaces, thereby improving computational efficiency and reducing complexity. Discrete tokenizers naturally align with the autoregressive paradigm but still lag behind continuous ones, limiting their adoption in multimodal systems. To address this, we propose \textbf{SFTok}, a discrete tokenizer that incorporates a multi-step iterative mechanism for precise reconstruction. By integrating \textbf{self-forcing guided visual reconstruction} and \textbf{debias-and-fitting training strategy}, SFTok resolves the training-inference inconsistency in multi-step process, significantly enhancing image reconstruction quality. At a high compression rate of only 64 tokens per image, SFTok achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on ImageNet (rFID = 1.21) and demonstrates exceptional performance in class-to-image generation tasks (gFID = 2.29).

keyInsights: A critical "training-inference inconsistency" in multi-step discrete tokenization methods leads to representational errors and hinders performance.
Simulating inference conditions during training by using the model's own predictions (SFVR) is crucial for effective knowledge transfer and reducing cumulative errors across iterative steps.
Multi-step conditional distribution prediction offers lower theoretical cross-entropy loss and higher accuracy bounds compared to single-step prediction, justifying iterative refinement.
originalProblem: Discrete image tokenizers historically exhibit inferior image reconstruction quality compared to continuous tokenizers, particularly at high compression rates.
Existing multi-step discrete prediction methods suffer from a "training-inference inconsistency," where the model is trained with ground truth but must rely on its own predictions during inference.
This performance gap limits the integration of discrete tokenizers into unified, efficient autoregressive multimodal training paradigms.
results: SFTok-L achieved an rFID of 1.21 at 64 tokens per image on ImageNet, establishing a new state-of-the-art for discrete tokenizers and surpassing many continuous tokenizers.
Integrated into MaskGIT, SFTok-L obtained a class-conditional generation gFID of 2.29, outperforming the original MaskGIT and several diffusion-based models.
Ablation studies confirmed the SFVR strategy and the warming-up procedure were essential for SFTok's improved reconstruction quality and training stability.
solution: SFTok introduces a multi-step iterative discrete tokenizer framework designed for precise image reconstruction.
It employs Self-Forcing Guided Visual Reconstruction (SFVR), a mask replacement strategy that feeds the model's own first-step prediction back during training, simulating inference conditions.

A three-stage debias-and-fitting training strategy, including warming up, distribution alignment with SFVR, and fine-tuning with adversarial and perceptual losses, enhances stability and reconstruction quality.
paper_summary.summary: SFTok, developed by Tsinghua University, presents a discrete tokenizer that achieves state-of-the-art image reconstruction quality and generative performance, making it competitive with or superior to continuous tokenizers. It obtains an rFID of 1.21 for its large model at a high compression rate of 64 tokens per image and a gFID of 2.29 in downstream image generation tasks, paving the way for unified autoregressive multimodal AI systems.

title: SFTok: Bridging the Performance Gap in Discrete Tokenizers

abstract: Video autoencoders compress videos into compact latent representations for efficient reconstruction, playing a vital role in enhancing the quality and efficiency of video generation. However, existing video autoencoders often entangle spatial and temporal information, limiting their ability to capture temporal consistency and leading to suboptimal performance. To address this, we propose Autoregressive Video Autoencoder (ARVAE), which compresses and reconstructs each frame conditioned on its predecessor in an autoregressive manner, allowing flexible processing of videos with arbitrary lengths. ARVAE introduces a temporal-spatial decoupled representation that combines downsampled flow field for temporal coherence with spatial relative compensation for newly emerged content, achieving high compression efficiency without information loss. Specifically, the encoder compresses the current and previous frames into the temporal motion and spatial supplement, while the decoder reconstructs the original frame from the latent representations given the preceding frame. A multi-stage training strategy is employed to progressively optimize the model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ARVAE achieves superior reconstruction quality with extremely lightweight models and small-scale training data. Moreover, evaluations on video generation tasks highlight its strong potential for downstream applications.

keyInsights: Decoupling temporal and spatial information into distinct latent representations effectively captures inter-frame motion and intra-frame novel content, enhancing reconstruction quality and temporal consistency.
The integration of multi-scale propagated features and dedicated 'state features' is crucial for maintaining contextual details and preventing quality degradation during long-term video propagation.

A progressive multi-stage training approach, starting with short sequences and incrementally extending them, significantly improves learning stability, convergence speed, and the model's robustness to variable video lengths.
originalProblem: Existing video autoencoders often entangle spatial and temporal information, limiting temporal consistency and efficient representation.
Many current video AEs are constrained to fixed-length video segments and suffer from large model parameters and extensive data requirements.
Challenges in achieving high compression efficiency without significant information loss across variable video lengths.
results: ARVAE achieved competitive or superior reconstruction quality (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) across various compression rates on standard benchmarks.
The model demonstrated exceptional efficiency, requiring an 80x reduction in model parameters and a 6,700x reduction in training samples compared to certain baselines like Step-Video.

ARVAE exhibited strong flexibility for arbitrary video lengths, showing only a marginal performance drop when inferring on longer sequences, and its latent space proved effective for competitive video generation (FVD scores) in downstream tasks.
solution: ARVAE utilizes an autoregressive autoencoder architecture that processes video frame-by-frame, conditioned on the previously reconstructed frame.
The core approach involves decoupling latent representations into 'Temporal Motion' (T) for inter-frame relationships and 'Spatial Supplement' (S) for intra-frame details.

A multi-stage training strategy, gradually increasing sequence length and incorporating random frame skipping, is used to learn long-term temporal dependencies and enable arbitrary video length processing.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Zoom Communications developed ARVAE, an autoregressive video autoencoder that employs decoupled temporal and spatial latent representations for efficient video compression and generation. The model achieves competitive or superior reconstruction quality, while using 80x fewer model parameters and 6,700x fewer training samples than some existing methods, and its latent space supports high-quality video synthesis.

title: Autoregressive Video Autoencoder with Decoupled Temporal and Spatial Context

abstract: The high dimensionality of images presents architecture and sampling-efficiency challenges for likelihood-based generative models. Previous approaches such as VQ-VAE use deep autoencoders to obtain compact representations, which are more practical as inputs for likelihood-based models. We present an alternative approach, inspired by common image compression methods like JPEG, and convert images to quantized discrete cosine transform (DCT) blocks, which are represented sparsely as a sequence of DCT channel, spatial location, and DCT coefficient triples. We propose a Transformer-based autoregressive architecture, which is trained to sequentially predict the conditional distribution of the next element in such sequences, and which scales effectively to high resolution images. On a range of image datasets, we demonstrate that our approach can generate high quality, diverse images, with sample metric scores competitive with state of the art methods. We additionally show that simple modifications to our method yield effective image colorization and super-resolution models.

keyInsights: Leveraging classical image compression techniques, specifically the Discrete Cosine Transform, provides an effective and interpretable sparse representation that bypasses the need for learned neural compression.

A chunked training approach and stacked decoder architecture allows Transformers to efficiently handle very long sparse sequences arising from high-resolution images, overcoming typical memory scaling issues.
Decomposing the prediction of (channel, position, value) into sequential sub-problems for stacked decoders improves the content modeled per sequence element and inference efficiency.
originalProblem: High dimensionality of image data presents computational and architectural challenges for likelihood-based generative models.
Existing neural compression schemes like VQ-VAE require training a separate, complex autoencoder.
Scaling autoregressive models to high resolutions (e.g., 256x256 and above) is limited by quadratic memory and compute costs for self-attention.
results: Achieved competitive FID scores and consistently better recall scores (indicating superior diversity) compared to state-of-the-art GANs (StyleGAN, BigGAN) and VQ-VAE2 on LSUN, FFHQ, and ImageNet datasets.
Generated high-quality unconditional images at long-side resolutions up to 2048, demonstrating scalability and robustness on diverse datasets like Plant Leaves.
Successfully adapted the core model for 8x image upsampling and image colorization, producing outputs with metrics closely matching ground truth distributions.
solution: Images are converted into sparse DCT coefficient representations by applying block DCT to YCbCr channels, quantizing, and serializing non-zero elements into (channel, position, value) tuples.
A Transformer-based autoregressive model, DCTransformer, predicts the next element in this sparse DCT sequence, ordered from low to high frequencies for coarse-to-fine generation.

A chunked training strategy uses an encoder to process a dense snapshot of prior context and stacked decoders for channel, position, and value predictions within a target chunk, enabling efficient scaling to long sequences.
paper_summary.summary: DeepMind researchers present DCTransformer, a Transformer-based autoregressive model that leverages sparse Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations of images to achieve high-quality and diverse image generation. The method generates images up to 2048 long-side resolution, achieving competitive FID and superior recall scores compared to state-of-the-art GANs, and can be adapted for image upsampling and colorization.

title: Generating Images with Sparse Representations

abstract: In generative modeling, numerous successful approaches leverage a low-dimensional latent space, e.g., Stable Diffusion models the latent space induced by an encoder and generates images through a paired decoder. Although the selection of the latent space is empirically pivotal, determining the optimal choice and the process of identifying it remain unclear. In this study, we aim to shed light on this under-explored topic by rethinking the latent space from the perspective of model complexity. Our investigation starts with the classic generative adversarial networks (GANs). Inspired by the GAN training objective, we propose a novel "distance" between the latent and data distributions, whose minimization coincides with that of the generator complexity. The minimizer of this distance is characterized as the optimal data-dependent latent that most effectively capitalizes on the generator's capacity. Then, we consider parameterizing such a latent distribution by an encoder network and propose a two-stage training strategy called Decoupled Autoencoder (DAE), where the encoder is only updated in the first stage with an auxiliary decoder and then frozen in the second stage while the actual decoder is being trained. DAE can improve the latent distribution and as a result, improve the generative performance. Our theoretical analyses are corroborated by comprehensive experiments on various models such as VQGAN and Diffusion Transformer, where our modifications yield significant improvements in sample quality with decreased model complexity.

organization_info.name: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

title: Complexity Matters: Rethinking the Latent Space for Generative Modeling

abstract: Visual tokenizers are fundamental to image generation. They convert visual data into discrete tokens, enabling transformer-based models to excel at image generation. Despite their success, VQ-based tokenizers like VQGAN face significant limitations due to constrained vocabulary sizes. Simply expanding the codebook often leads to training instability and diminishing performance gains, making scalability a critical challenge. In this work, we introduce Factorized Quantization (FQ), a novel approach that revitalizes VQ-based tokenizers by decomposing a large codebook into multiple independent sub-codebooks. This factorization reduces the lookup complexity of large codebooks, enabling more efficient and scalable visual tokenization. To ensure each sub-codebook captures distinct and complementary information, we propose a disentanglement regularization that explicitly reduces redundancy, promoting diversity across the sub-codebooks. Furthermore, we integrate representation learning into the training process, leveraging pretrained vision models like CLIP and DINO to infuse semantic richness into the learned representations. This design ensures our tokenizer captures diverse semantic levels, leading to more expressive and disentangled representations. Experiments show that the proposed FQGAN model substantially improves the reconstruction quality of visual tokenizers, achieving state-of-the-art performance. We further demonstrate that this tokenizer can be effectively adapted into auto-regressive image generation. this https URL
title: Factorized Visual Tokenization and Generation

abstract: In this work, we present HieraTok, a novel multi-scale Vision Transformer (ViT)-based tokenizer that overcomes the inherent limitation of modeling single-scale representations. This is realized through two key designs: (1) multi-scale downsampling applied to the token map generated by the tokenizer encoder, producing a sequence of multi-scale tokens, and (2) a scale-causal attention mechanism that enables the progressive flow of information from low-resolution global semantic features to high-resolution structural details. Coupling these designs, HieraTok achieves significant improvements in both image reconstruction and generation tasks. Under identical settings, the multi-scale visual tokenizer outperforms its single-scale counterpart by a 27.2\% improvement in rFID ($1.47 \rightarrow 1.07$). When integrated into downstream generation frameworks, it achieves a $1.38\times$ faster convergence rate and an 18.9\% boost in gFID ($16.4 \rightarrow 13.3$), which may be attributed to the smoother and more uniformly distributed latent space. Furthermore, by scaling up the tokenizer's training, we demonstrate its potential by a sota rFID of 0.45 and a gFID of 1.82 among ViT tokenizers. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to introduce multi-scale ViT-based tokenizer in image reconstruction and image generation. We hope our findings and designs advance the ViT-based tokenizers in visual generation tasks.

keyInsights: Integrating multi-scale capabilities into ViT-based tokenizers significantly enhances both image reconstruction and downstream image generation performance.
Learnable convolutional downsampling, as opposed to simple interpolation, is critical for constructing effective multi-scale tokens that improve generation quality.
The scale-causal attention mechanism effectively establishes a hierarchical information flow, leading to a more structured and generation-friendly latent space.
originalProblem: Traditional ViT tokenizers operate on fixed-length token sequences, limiting their ability to capture multi-scale visual information.
The global attention mechanism in standard ViT tokenizers lacks inductive biases for hierarchical relationships, hindering the natural flow from global semantics to local details.
Existing ViT tokenizers often produce non-uniform or over-concentrated latent spaces, which can impede the training speed and final quality of downstream generative models.
results: HieraTok achieved a 27.2% reduction in rFID and a 19.4% improvement in LPIPS for image reconstruction compared to single-scale baselines.
In image generation tasks, it demonstrated 1.38x faster convergence and an 18.9% boost in gFID (from 16.4 to 13.3), along with a substantial IS increase (57.4 to 68.5) under identical training steps.
The method established a state-of-the-art rFID of 0.45 and a highly competitive gFID of 1.82 among ViT-based tokenizers, producing a smoother and more uniformly distributed latent space.
solution: HieraTok introduces multi-scale token construction using learnable convolutional downsampling to create hierarchical feature maps.

It incorporates a novel scale-causal attention mechanism in the decoder, allowing tokens at higher resolutions to interact only with tokens from the same or lower resolutions, fostering progressive refinement.
Multi-scale RGB supervision is applied, requiring each scale's token map to reconstruct the corresponding downsampled ground truth image, enforcing scale consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University and Ant Group developed HieraTok, a multi-scale Vision Transformer (ViT)-based tokenizer that improves image reconstruction fidelity and accelerates image generation by creating a more uniformly distributed latent space. The tokenizer achieved a state-of-the-art rFID of 0.45 and a competitive gFID of 1.82.

title: HieraTok: Multi-Scale Visual Tokenizer Improves Image Reconstruction and Generation

abstract: Visual generative models based on latent space have achieved great success, underscoring the significance of visual tokenization. Mapping images to latents boosts efficiency and enables multimodal alignment for scaling up in downstream tasks. Existing visual tokenizers primarily map images into fixed 2D spatial grids and focus on pixel-level restoration, which hinders the capture of representations with compact global semantics. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{SemTok}, a semantic one-dimensional tokenizer that compresses 2D images into 1D discrete tokens with high-level semantics. SemTok sets a new state-of-the-art in image reconstruction, achieving superior fidelity with a remarkably compact token representation. This is achieved via a synergistic framework with three key innovations: a 2D-to-1D tokenization scheme, a semantic alignment constraint, and a two-stage generative training strategy. Building on SemTok, we construct a masked autoregressive generation framework, which yields notable improvements in downstream image generation tasks. Experiments confirm the effectiveness of our semantic 1D tokenization. Our code will be open-sourced.

keyInsights: Explicit semantic alignment constraints, incorporating both spatial distillation and contrastive learning, are crucial for guiding the encoder to produce compact, clustered, and semantically rich 1D representations.

A two-stage generative training paradigm, combining diffusion-based pre-training with refinement fine-tuning, is more effective than direct pixel-level optimization for exploring diverse latent spaces and enhancing both semantic understanding and fine-grained fidelity.

Non-sequential masked autoregressive modeling for 1D tokens effectively leverages their global semantic complementarity, outperforming causal, sequential prediction in downstream image generation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing 2D grid-based visual tokenizers focus on local pixel details, leading to redundant information and hindering the capture of compact, globally-aware image semantics.
Tokenizers optimized for pixel-level reconstruction often produce latent spaces lacking high-level semantic discriminability, resulting in low-information density and flattened distributions.
Training solely on pixel-level reconstruction can lead to oversmoothed latent spaces and distribution collapse, limiting the diversity of generated outputs in generative models.
results: SemTok achieved state-of-the-art image reconstruction on ImageNet-1k, with an rFID of 0.67 at 0.125 Bits-per-pixel (BPP), surpassing previous 2D and 1D tokenizers including SD-v2.1's VAE tokenizer.
Integrated into a masked autoregressive framework, SemTok-AR-XXL (1.2B parameters) demonstrated competitive class-conditional image generation with a gFID of 2.34 and IS of 310.5 on ImageNet-1k.

Ablation studies confirmed that the semantic alignment constraint, two-stage generative training, and non-sequential masked autoregressive modeling each significantly improved both image reconstruction and generation performance.
solution: Introduced a Semantic One-Dimensional Tokenizer (SemTok) framework that uses an MMDiT-based encoder for 2D-to-1D conversion, supervised by a semantic alignment constraint leveraging a pre-trained SigLIP encoder.

Employed a Binary Spherical Quantizer (BSQ) to map continuous latents to discrete vectors, allowing for exponentially growing codebook sizes without explicit storage and facilitating semantic disentanglement.
Implemented a two-stage generative training strategy: initial diffusion-based generative pre-training followed by refinement-oriented fine-tuning with pixel-level reconstruction and GAN losses.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Alibaba Group developed SemTok, a visual tokenizer that converts 2D images into compact, semantically rich 1D discrete token sequences. This framework achieves state-of-the-art image reconstruction, with an rFID of 0.67 at 0.125 BPP, and enables high-quality class-conditional image generation with a gFID of 2.34.

title: Semantic One-Dimensional Tokenizer for Image Reconstruction and Generation

abstract: Tokenized visual representations have shown promise in image compression, yet their extension to video remains underexplored due to the challenges posed by complex temporal dynamics and stringent bit rate constraints. In this paper, we present tokenized video compression (TVC), a token-based dual-stream framework designed to operate effectively at ultra-low bit rates. TVC leverages the Cosmos video tokenizer to extract both discrete and continuous token streams. The discrete tokens are partially masked using a strategic masking scheme and then compressed losslessly with a discrete checkerboard context model to reduce transmission overhead. The masked tokens are reconstructed by a decoder-only Transformer with spatiotemporal token prediction. In parallel, the continuous tokens are quantized and compressed using a continuous checkerboard context model, providing complementary continuous information at ultra-low bit rates. At the decoder side, the two streams are fused with a ControlNet-based multi-scale integration module, ensuring high perceptual quality alongside stable fidelity in reconstruction. Overall, this work illustrates the practicality of tokenized video compression and points to new directions for semantics-aware, token-native approaches.
title: TVC: Tokenized Video Compression with Ultra-Low Bit Rate

abstract: Video tokenizers are essential for latent video diffusion models, converting raw video data into spatiotemporally compressed latent spaces for efficient training. However, extending state-of-the-art video tokenizers to achieve a temporal compression ratio beyond 4x without increasing channel capacity poses significant challenges. In this work, we propose an alternative approach to enhance temporal compression. We find that the reconstruction quality of temporally subsampled videos from a low-compression encoder surpasses that of high-compression encoders applied to original videos. This indicates that high-compression models can leverage representations from lower-compression models. Building on this insight, we develop a bootstrapped high-temporal-compression model that progressively trains high-compression blocks atop well-trained lower-compression models. Our method includes a cross-level feature-mixing module to retain information from the pretrained low-compression model and guide higher-compression blocks to capture the remaining details from the full video sequence. Evaluation of video benchmarks shows that our method significantly improves reconstruction quality while increasing temporal compression compared to directly training the full model. Furthermore, the resulting compact latent space effectively trains a video diffusion model for high-quality video generation with a significantly reduced token budget.
title: Progressive Growing of Video Tokenizers for Temporally Compact Latent Spaces

abstract: Flexible image tokenizers aim to represent an image using an ordered 1D variable-length token sequence. This flexible tokenization is typically achieved through nested dropout, where a portion of trailing tokens is randomly truncated during training, and the image is reconstructed using the remaining preceding sequence. However, this tail-truncation strategy inherently concentrates the image information in the early tokens, limiting the effectiveness of downstream AutoRegressive (AR) image generation as the token length increases. To overcome these limitations, we propose \textbf{ReToK}, a flexible tokenizer with \underline{Re}dundant \underline{Tok}en Padding and Hierarchical Semantic Regularization, designed to fully exploit all tokens for enhanced latent modeling. Specifically, we introduce \textbf{Redundant Token Padding} to activate tail tokens more frequently, thereby alleviating information over-concentration in the early tokens. In addition, we apply \textbf{Hierarchical Semantic Regularization} to align the decoding features of earlier tokens with those from a pre-trained vision foundation model, while progressively reducing the regularization strength toward the tail to allow finer low-level detail reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of ReTok: on ImageNet 256$\times$256, our method achieves superior generation performance compared with both flexible and fixed-length tokenizers. Code will be available at: \href{this https URL}{this https URL}

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: Naive nested dropout causes information over-concentration in early tokens, leading to the generation bottleneck in flexible tokenizers.
Proactively activating tail tokens through methods like redundant padding is crucial for enabling consistent quality improvement as token sequence length increases.

Adapting semantic regularization strength based on token position (hierarchical regularization) effectively balances the capture of high-level semantics by early tokens and fine-grained details by later tokens.
originalProblem: Existing flexible image tokenizers, using naive nested dropout, suffer from a "generation bottleneck" where downstream autoregressive models do not consistently improve image quality with increasing token sequence length.
Most essential image information becomes over-concentrated in the initial tokens of a sequence, leaving later or "tail" tokens under-utilized or even redundant.
This limitation prevents flexible tokenizers from fully exploiting their variable-length potential and achieving performance comparable to state-of-the-art fixed-length tokenizers.
results: ReTok successfully resolves the generation bottleneck, achieving consistent improvements in generation quality (gFID) as the number of tokens increases from 32 to 256.
The method achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art fixed-length tokenizers, with ReTok-S-B achieving a gFID of 2.27 on ImageNet 256x256, outperforming other flexible tokenizers.

Ablation studies confirm Redundant Token Padding significantly activates tail tokens, leading to more uniform information contribution, while Hierarchical Semantic Regularization enhances both short and long sequence generation quality.
solution: Redundant Token Padding (RTP) is introduced to force the tokenizer to distribute information more evenly across original tokens by training on extended sequences that include additional padding tokens.

Hierarchical Semantic Regularization (HSR) employs a varying regularization weight for semantic alignment, guiding early tokens to capture high-level semantics and later tokens to focus on fine-grained details.
A decoder fine-tuning step is applied after the main training phase to further improve reconstruction performance across all token lengths.
paper_summary.summary: ReTok, developed by researchers including those from Nanyang Technological University and Meta AI, improves flexible image tokenizers by resolving a "generation bottleneck" where image quality fails to consistently improve with more tokens. The method activates tail tokens and applies hierarchical semantic regularization, leading to consistent quality gains and competitive performance in autoregressive image generation.

title: Improving Flexible Image Tokenizers for Autoregressive Image Generation

abstract: Continuous image tokenizers enable efficient visual generation, and those based on variational frameworks can learn smooth, structured latent representations through KL regularization. Yet this often leads to posterior collapse when using fewer tokens, where the encoder fails to encode informative features into the compressed latent space. To address this, we introduce \textbf{MacTok}, a \textbf{M}asked \textbf{A}ugmenting 1D \textbf{C}ontinuous \textbf{Tok}enizer that leverages image masking and representation alignment to prevent collapse while learning compact and robust representations. MacTok applies both random masking to regularize latent learning and DINO-guided semantic masking to emphasize informative regions in images, forcing the model to encode robust semantics from incomplete visual evidence. Combined with global and local representation alignment, MacTok preserves rich discriminative information in a highly compressed 1D latent space, requiring only 64 or 128 tokens. On ImageNet, MacTok achieves a competitive gFID of 1.44 at 256$\times$256 and a state-of-the-art 1.52 at 512$\times$512 with SiT-XL, while reducing token usage by up to 64$\times$. These results confirm that masking and semantic guidance together prevent posterior collapse and achieve efficient, high-fidelity tokenization.

keyInsights: Image-level masking, particularly with semantic guidance, fundamentally mitigates posterior collapse by forcing the latent space to retain essential and semantically discriminative information.
Multi-level representation alignment with pre-trained vision models (DINOv2) is crucial for organizing the latent space, enabling semantically similar concepts to cluster even with high compression.
It is possible to achieve state-of-the-art generative performance with significantly fewer tokens (e.g., 64 or 128) by learning robust, semantically rich continuous latent representations.
originalProblem: KL-based continuous image tokenizers frequently suffer from 'posterior collapse' under strong compression, leading to degenerate latent spaces that lose meaningful semantics and result in poor reconstruction and generation quality.
Existing solutions to posterior collapse, such as delicate KL weight tuning, have not provided a fundamental resolution to latent degeneration.

Prior representation alignment approaches in state-of-the-art tokenizers often rely on fixed token counts, perform coarse global alignment, or require multiple auxiliary objectives, limiting flexibility and consistency.
results: Achieved a gFID of 1.52 and an IS of 316.0 on ImageNet 512x512 with 128 tokens (SiT-XL and CFG), establishing a new state-of-the-art for image generation.
Demonstrated superior generation with fewer tokens, outperforming SoftVQ-VAE by 2.21 gFID on ImageNet 256x256 (64 tokens, without CFG).

Exhibited strong reconstruction performance with rFID scores of 0.43 for 128 tokens and 0.75 for 64 tokens on ImageNet 256x256, competitive with or superior to VQ-based tokenizers requiring many more tokens.
solution: MacTok employs a Vision Transformer-based continuous tokenizer architecture that uses a 1D latent space for flexible token counts, preventing posterior collapse through enforced information flow.
Dual image masking strategies, including random and DINO-guided semantic masking, compel the latent tokens to infer complete information and capture robust semantic features from partial inputs.

A novel global and local representation alignment mechanism connects each latent token with both holistic image semantics and specific regional DINOv2 features, structuring the latent space effectively.
paper_summary.summary: MacTok, a continuous tokenizer from Fudan University, robustly learns compact and semantically rich 1D latent representations by addressing posterior collapse in KL-based VAEs. It achieves a new state-of-the-art gFID of 1.52 on ImageNet 512x512 with 128 tokens and competitive reconstruction fidelity while using significantly fewer tokens.

title: MacTok: Robust Continuous Tokenization for Image Generation

abstract: Recent studies in extreme image compression have achieved remarkable performance by compressing the tokens from generative tokenizers. However, these methods often prioritize clustering common semantics within the dataset, while overlooking the diverse details of individual objects. Consequently, this results in suboptimal reconstruction fidelity, especially at low bitrates. To address this issue, we introduce a Dual-generative Latent Fusion (DLF) paradigm. DLF decomposes the latent into semantic and detail elements, compressing them through two distinct branches. The semantic branch clusters high-level information into compact tokens, while the detail branch encodes perceptually critical details to enhance the overall fidelity. Additionally, we propose a cross-branch interactive design to reduce redundancy between the two branches, thereby minimizing the overall bit cost. Experimental results demonstrate the impressive reconstruction quality of DLF even below 0.01 bits per pixel (bpp). On the CLIC2020 test set, our method achieves bitrate savings of up to 27.93% on LPIPS and 53.55% on DISTS compared to MS-ILLM. Furthermore, DLF surpasses recent diffusion-based codecs in visual fidelity while maintaining a comparable level of generative realism. Project: this https URL

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Decomposing latent representations into distinct semantic and detail components allows for a more flexible and adaptive compression strategy, addressing both dataset-level commonalities and unique individual object features.
An intelligent cross-branch interaction mechanism is essential for minimizing redundancy between parallel information streams and enabling complementary learning, leading to optimal bit allocation.

A hybrid quantization strategy, combining Vector Quantization for semantic tokens and spatially adaptive Scalar Quantization for details, proves critical for achieving superior fidelity by accommodating diverse detail characteristics.
originalProblem: Existing extreme image compression methods often produce blurry reconstructions or struggle to maintain perceptual quality and fidelity at ultra-low bitrates (below 0.01 bpp).
Single codebook tokenizer-based codecs prioritize dataset-level semantics, overlooking diverse, unique details of individual objects, leading to suboptimal reconstruction fidelity.

Achieving a balance between efficient encoding of high-level semantic information and faithful representation of fine-grained, unique details without introducing redundancy in multi-branch systems remains a challenge.
results: DLF achieved state-of-the-art compression performance on Kodak, CLIC2020, and MS-COCO 30K datasets, particularly at bitrates below 0.01 bpp.

It demonstrated substantial BD-rate savings compared to generative codecs like MS-ILLM (e.g., 43.05% for LPIPS and 67.82% for DISTS on Kodak), surpassing both tokenizer-based and diffusion-based methods in fidelity metrics.

The framework offers significantly faster decoding speeds (10-16 times faster) than diffusion-based codecs while maintaining competitive generative realism, even outperforming some diffusion models in FID on specific datasets.
solution: A dual-branch coding pipeline is introduced, decomposing image latent representation into semantic (compressed via VQ) and detail (compressed via spatially adaptive SQ with quadtree entropy modeling) components.

A cross-branch interactive design, featuring Interactive Transform (IT) modules, dynamically aggregates and reallocates information between branches to minimize redundancy and enhance complementary learning.

A two-stage training strategy, comprising latent alignment followed by end-to-end fine-tuning with a comprehensive generative loss, optimizes for both latent fidelity and pixel-domain perceptual quality.
paper_summary.summary: DLF, a novel dual-generative latent fusion framework, achieves state-of-the-art extreme image compression by decomposing latent features into semantic and detail components, leading to superior perceptual quality and reconstruction fidelity at bitrates below 0.01 bpp. It demonstrates BD-rate savings of 43.05% for LPIPS and 67.82% for DISTS compared to MS-ILLM on the Kodak dataset, while offering significantly faster decoding than diffusion models.

title: DLF: Extreme Image Compression with Dual-generative Latent Fusion

abstract: Autoencoders empower state-of-the-art image and video generative models by compressing pixels into a latent space through visual tokenization. Although recent advances have alleviated the performance degradation of autoencoders under high compression ratios, addressing the training instability caused by GAN remains an open challenge. While improving spatial compression, we also aim to minimize the latent space dimensionality, enabling more efficient and compact representations. To tackle these challenges, we focus on improving the decoder's expressiveness. Concretely, we propose DGAE, which employs a diffusion model to guide the decoder in recovering informative signals that are not fully decoded from the latent representation. With this design, DGAE effectively mitigates the performance degradation under high spatial compression rates. At the same time, DGAE achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 2x smaller latent space. When integrated with Diffusion Models, DGAE demonstrates competitive performance on image generation for ImageNet-1K and shows that this compact latent representation facilitates faster convergence of the diffusion model.
title: DGAE: Diffusion-Guided Autoencoder for Efficient Latent Representation Learning

abstract: Latent generative models have shown remarkable progress in high-fidelity image synthesis, typically using a two-stage training process that involves compressing images into latent embeddings via learned tokenizers in the first stage. The quality of generation strongly depends on how expressive and well-optimized these latent embeddings are. While various methods have been proposed to learn effective latent representations, generated images often lack realism, particularly in textured regions with sharp transitions, due to loss of fine details governed by high frequencies. We conduct a detailed frequency decomposition of existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) latent tokenizers and show that conventional objectives inherently prioritize low-frequency reconstruction, often at the expense of high-frequency fidelity. Our analysis reveals these latent tokenizers exhibit a bias toward low-frequency information during optimization, leading to over-smoothed outputs and visual artifacts that diminish perceptual quality. To address this, we propose a wavelet-based, frequency-aware variational autoencoder (FA-VAE) framework that explicitly decouples the optimization of low- and high-frequency components. This decoupling enables improved reconstruction of fine textures while preserving global structure. Moreover, we integrate our frequency-preserving latent embeddings into a SOTA latent diffusion model, resulting in sharper and more realistic image generation. Our approach bridges the fidelity gap in current latent tokenizers and emphasizes the importance of frequency-aware optimization for realistic image synthesis, with broader implications for applications in content creation, neural rendering, and medical imaging.

keyInsights: A systematic frequency-based analysis reveals that current state-of-the-art latent tokenizers consistently exhibit a bias towards reconstructing low-frequency information while substantially under-preserving high-frequency details.

Explicitly decoupling and optimizing low- and high-frequency components during latent space learning is crucial for comprehensive detail preservation, outperforming methods that jointly optimize these components.

Addressing frequency degradation at the foundational latent representation level offers a more fundamental and generalizable improvement for generative models, impacting any downstream generative process.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art latent generative models, particularly Latent Diffusion Models, often generate images lacking sharp textures and fine details, diminishing perceptual realism.
Existing latent tokenizers implicitly prioritize the reconstruction of low-frequency information, leading to poor preservation of high-frequency content during image compression.
Many prior approaches attempt to compensate for frequency degradation during the generation phase, rather than addressing its root cause in the initial latent representation learning.
results: FA-VAE achieved state-of-the-art reconstruction performance, nearly halving the overall reconstruction loss (0.0044) and dramatically reducing high-frequency loss (0.0020) compared to the best baseline (VA-VAE's 0.0105 and 0.0074, respectively).
When integrated into LightningDiT, FA-VAE embeddings yielded superior generative quality, achieving the best gFID (1.32) and improved IS (317.4) among compared models.

Qualitative results demonstrate that FA-VAE reconstructions and generations are visually sharper, retain more fine details and text, and exhibit more balanced fidelity across diverse image categories, indicating a 'fairer' latent space.
solution: The FA-VAE framework utilizes Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) to explicitly decompose input images into distinct low-frequency ($x_L$) and high-frequency ($x_H$) components.
It employs dedicated, decoupled encoder-decoder pairs for each frequency band: $(E_L, D_L)$ for low frequencies and $(E_H, D_H)$ for high frequencies, optimized with frequency-specific objectives.

The learned frequency-aware latent codes ($z_L, z_H$) are fused via concatenation and then used as input to existing latent diffusion models (e.g., LightningDiT) without requiring architectural modifications.
paper_summary.summary: Medi et al. introduce the Frequency-Aware VAE (FA-VAE) framework, designed to overcome the loss of fine details and high-frequency information prevalent in images produced by latent generative models. This method explicitly decouples and optimizes low- and high-frequency components during latent representation learning, resulting in significantly sharper, more realistic, and perceptually superior image synthesis.

title: Missing Fine Details in Images: Last Seen in High Frequencies

abstract: Visual tokenizers play a central role in latent image generation by bridging high-dimensional images and tractable generative modeling. However, most existing tokenizers are still trained with reconstruction-dominated objectives, which often yield latent representations that are only weakly grounded in high-level semantics. Recent approaches improve semantic alignment, but typically treat semantic signals as auxiliary regularization rather than making them functionally necessary for representation learning. We propose SMAP, a SeMantic-Aware Prefix tokenizer that injects class-level semantic conditions into a query-based 1D tokenization framework. To make semantics indispensable during training, SMAP introduces a tail token dropping strategy, which forces semantic conditions and early latent prefixes to bear increasing responsibility under progressively reduced token budgets. To verify that the resulting latent space is useful for generation rather than reconstruction alone, we further introduce CARD, a hybrid Causal AutoRegressive--Diffusion generator. Extensive experiments on ImageNet show that SMAP consistently improves reconstruction quality across discrete and continuous tokenization settings, and that its semantically grounded latent space yields strong downstream generation performance under compact token budgets.
title: Semantic-Aware Prefix Learning for Token-Efficient Image Generation

abstract: Vision Transformer (ViT) based autoencoders often underutilize the global Class token and employ static attention mechanisms, limiting both generative control and optimization efficiency. This paper introduces ViTCAE, a framework that addresses these issues by re-purposing the Class token into a generative linchpin. In our architecture, the encoder maps the Class token to a global latent variable that dictates the prior distribution for local, patch-level latent variables, establishing a robust dependency where global semantics directly inform the synthesis of local details. Drawing inspiration from opinion dynamics, we treat each attention head as a dynamical system of interacting tokens seeking consensus. This perspective motivates a convergence-aware temperature scheduler that adaptively anneals each head's influence function based on its distributional stability. This process enables a principled head-freezing mechanism, guided by theoretically-grounded diagnostics like an attention evolution distance and a consensus/cluster functional. This technique prunes converged heads during training to significantly improve computational efficiency without sacrificing fidelity. By unifying a generative Class token with an adaptive attention mechanism rooted in multi-agent consensus theory, ViTCAE offers a more efficient and controllable approach to transformer-based generation.
title: ViTCAE: ViT-based Class-conditioned Autoencoder

abstract: The quality of the latent space in visual tokenizers (e.g., VAEs) is crucial for modern generative models. However, the standard reconstruction-based training paradigm produces a latent space that is biased towards low-level information, leading to a foundation flaw: better pixel-level accuracy does not lead to higher-quality generation. This implies that pouring extensive compute into visual tokenizer pre-training translates poorly to improved performance in generation. We identify this as the ``pre-training scaling problem`` and suggest a necessary shift: to be effective for generation, a latent space must concisely represent high-level semantics. We present VTP, a unified visual tokenizer pre-training framework, pioneering the joint optimization of image-text contrastive, self-supervised, and reconstruction losses. Our large-scale study reveals two principal findings: (1) understanding is a key driver of generation, and (2) much better scaling properties, where generative performance scales effectively with compute, parameters, and data allocated to the pretraining of the visual tokenizer. After large-scale pre-training, our tokenizer delivers a competitive profile (78.2 zero-shot accuracy and 0.36 rFID on ImageNet) and 4.1 times faster convergence on generation compared to advanced distillation methods. More importantly, it scales effectively: without modifying standard DiT training specs, solely investing more FLOPS in pretraining VTP achieves 65.8\% FID improvement in downstream generation, while conventional autoencoder stagnates very early at 1/10 FLOPS. Our pre-trained models are available at this https URL.
title: Towards Scalable Pre-training of Visual Tokenizers for Generation

abstract: Image tokenization plays a central role in modern generative modeling by mapping visual inputs into compact representations that serve as an intermediate signal between pixels and generative models. Diffusion-based decoders have recently been adopted in image tokenization to reconstruct images from latent representations with high perceptual fidelity. In contrast to diffusion models used for downstream generation, these decoders are dedicated to faithful reconstruction rather than content generation. However, their iterative sampling process introduces significant latency, making them impractical for real-time or large-scale applications. In this work, we introduce a two-stage acceleration framework to address this inefficiency. First, we propose a multi-scale sampling strategy, where decoding begins at a coarse resolution and progressively refines the output by doubling the resolution at each stage, achieving a theoretical speedup of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ compared to standard full-resolution sampling. Second, we distill the diffusion decoder at each scale into a single-step denoising model, enabling fast and high-quality reconstructions in a single forward pass per scale. Together, these techniques yield an order-of-magnitude reduction in decoding time with little degradation in output quality. Our approach provides a practical pathway toward efficient yet expressive image tokenizers. We hope it serves as a foundation for future work in efficient visual tokenization and downstream generation.

keyInsights: Distributing the diffusion denoising process across multiple spatial scales (coarse-to-fine) provides a substantial 10x speedup and improves reconstruction fidelity compared to single-scale decoding.
One-step distillation per scale, building upon the multi-scale teacher, offers a further 31x speedup, effectively condensing iterative denoising into single forward passes.

Optimal performance for the distilled model is achieved with a moderate perceptual loss weight (λ_perc = 0.5) and a classifier-free guidance scale of 1, indicating sensitivity to these hyperparameters in single-step generation.
originalProblem: Diffusion decoders, despite offering high perceptual fidelity for image reconstruction, are computationally expensive due to their iterative denoising process, requiring tens to hundreds of steps.
This slow inference speed limits their practical application in real-time or large-scale systems for image tokenization.
Existing deterministic decoders for image tokenization are faster but often struggle to synthesize fine details, leading to blurriness or artifacts.
results: The distilled multi-scale diffusion decoder achieved a throughput of 87.16 images/second on ImageNet-1K, representing an approximately 459x speedup compared to DiTo and 60x over FlowMo.

Reconstruction quality remained competitive with an rFID of 1.09, PSNR of 24.74, and SSIM of 0.800, generally outperforming or matching non-diffusion tokenizers while offering significantly higher speeds than other diffusion-based approaches.

Ablation studies confirmed that multi-scale denoising improved speed by 10x and quality, while distillation provided an additional 31x speedup with a slight rFID increase from 0.91 (teacher) to 1.09 (student).
solution: A two-stage acceleration framework is proposed, integrating multi-scale sampling with one-step distillation for diffusion decoders.
Stage 1 trains a multi-scale conditional diffusion decoder, reconstructing images progressively from low to high resolutions (e.g., 32x32 to 256x256 pixels) using a flow-matching objective.

Stage 2 distills this multi-scale teacher into a student model that performs single-step denoising per scale, utilizing multi-scale reconstruction, perceptual (LPIPS), and adversarial (DINO-based) losses.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UC San Diego and Carnegie Mellon University developed a two-stage framework that combines multi-scale sampling with one-step distillation to accelerate diffusion decoders. The method achieves an approximately 459x speedup in image reconstruction, processing 87 images per second, while largely preserving high perceptual fidelity on the ImageNet-1K dataset.

title: Accelerating Diffusion Decoders via Multi-Scale Sampling and One-Step Distillation

abstract: Recent work leverages Vision Foundation Models as image encoders to boost the generative performance of latent diffusion models (LDMs), as their semantic feature distributions are easy to learn. However, such semantic features often lack low-level information (\eg, color and texture), leading to degraded reconstruction fidelity, which has emerged as a primary bottleneck in further scaling LDMs. To address this limitation, we propose LV-RAE, a representation autoencoder that augments semantic features with missing low-level information, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction while remaining highly aligned with the semantic distribution. We further observe that the resulting high-dimensional, information-rich latent make decoders sensitive to latent perturbations, causing severe artifacts when decoding generated latent and consequently degrading generation quality. Our analysis suggests that this sensitivity primarily stems from excessive decoder responses along directions off the data manifold. Building on these insights, we propose fine-tuning the decoder to increase its robustness and smoothing the generated latent via controlled noise injection, thereby enhancing generation quality. Experiments demonstrate that LV-RAE significantly improves reconstruction fidelity while preserving the semantic abstraction and achieving strong generative quality. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: Improving Reconstruction of Representation Autoencoder

abstract: Autoregressive visual generation models typically rely on tokenizers to compress images into tokens that can be predicted sequentially. A fundamental dilemma exists in token representation: discrete tokens enable straightforward modeling with standard cross-entropy loss, but suffer from information loss and tokenizer training instability; continuous tokens better preserve visual details, but require complex distribution modeling, complicating the generation pipeline. In this paper, we propose TokenBridge, which bridges this gap by maintaining the strong representation capacity of continuous tokens while preserving the modeling simplicity of discrete tokens. To achieve this, we decouple discretization from the tokenizer training process through post-training quantization that directly obtains discrete tokens from continuous representations. Specifically, we introduce a dimension-wise quantization strategy that independently discretizes each feature dimension, paired with a lightweight autoregressive prediction mechanism that efficiently model the resulting large token space. Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves reconstruction and generation quality on par with continuous methods while using standard categorical prediction. This work demonstrates that bridging discrete and continuous paradigms can effectively harness the strengths of both approaches, providing a promising direction for high-quality visual generation with simple autoregressive modeling. Project page: this https URL.
title: Bridging Continuous and Discrete Tokens for Autoregressive Visual Generation

abstract: Latent generative models have shown remarkable progress in high-fidelity image synthesis, typically using a two-stage training process that involves compressing images into latent embeddings via learned tokenizers in the first stage. The quality of generation strongly depends on how expressive and well-optimized these latent embeddings are. While various methods have been proposed to learn effective latent representations, generated images often lack realism, particularly in textured regions with sharp transitions, due to loss of fine details governed by high frequencies. We conduct a detailed frequency decomposition of existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) latent tokenizers and show that conventional objectives inherently prioritize low-frequency reconstruction, often at the expense of high-frequency fidelity. Our analysis reveals these latent tokenizers exhibit a bias toward low-frequency information during optimization, leading to over-smoothed outputs and visual artifacts that diminish perceptual quality. To address this, we propose a wavelet-based, frequency-aware variational autoencoder (FA-VAE) framework that explicitly decouples the optimization of low- and high-frequency components. This decoupling enables improved reconstruction of fine textures while preserving global structure. Moreover, we integrate our frequency-preserving latent embeddings into a SOTA latent diffusion model, resulting in sharper and more realistic image generation. Our approach bridges the fidelity gap in current latent tokenizers and emphasizes the importance of frequency-aware optimization for realistic image synthesis, with broader implications for applications in content creation, neural rendering, and medical imaging.
title: Missing Fine Details in Images: Last Seen in High Frequencies

abstract: Image tokenization has significantly advanced visual generation and multimodal modeling, particularly when paired with autoregressive models. However, current methods face challenges in balancing efficiency and fidelity: high-resolution image reconstruction either requires an excessive number of tokens or compromises critical details through token reduction. To resolve this, we propose Latent Consistency Tokenizer (Layton) that bridges discrete visual tokens with the compact latent space of pre-trained Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), enabling efficient representation of 1024x1024 images using only 256 tokens-a 16 times compression over VQGAN. Layton integrates a transformer encoder, a quantized codebook, and a latent consistency decoder. Direct application of LDM as the decoder results in color and brightness discrepancies. Thus, we convert it to latent consistency decoder, reducing multi-step sampling to 1-2 steps for direct pixel-level supervision. Experiments demonstrate Layton's superiority in high-fidelity reconstruction, with 10.8 reconstruction Frechet Inception Distance on MSCOCO-2017 5K benchmark for 1024x1024 image reconstruction. We also extend Layton to a text-to-image generation model, LaytonGen, working in autoregression. It achieves 0.73 score on GenEval benchmark, surpassing current state-of-the-art methods. Project homepage: this https URL
title: Layton: Latent Consistency Tokenizer for 1024-pixel Image Reconstruction and Generation by 256 Tokens

abstract: Since the advent of popular visual generation frameworks like VQGAN and latent diffusion models, state-of-the-art image generation systems have generally been two-stage systems that first tokenize or compress visual data into a lower-dimensional latent space before learning a generative model. Tokenizer training typically follows a standard recipe in which images are compressed and reconstructed subject to a combination of MSE, perceptual, and adversarial losses. Diffusion autoencoders have been proposed in prior work as a way to learn end-to-end perceptually-oriented image compression, but have not yet shown state-of-the-art performance on the competitive task of ImageNet-1K reconstruction. We propose FlowMo, a transformer-based diffusion autoencoder that achieves a new state-of-the-art for image tokenization at multiple compression rates without using convolutions, adversarial losses, spatially-aligned two-dimensional latent codes, or distilling from other tokenizers. Our key insight is that FlowMo training should be broken into a mode-matching pre-training stage and a mode-seeking post-training stage. In addition, we conduct extensive analyses and explore the training of generative models atop the FlowMo tokenizer. Our code and models will be available at this http URL .
title: Flow to the Mode: Mode-Seeking Diffusion Autoencoders for State-of-the-Art Image Tokenization

abstract: We present DC-AE 1.5, a new family of deep compression autoencoders for high-resolution diffusion models. Increasing the autoencoder's latent channel number is a highly effective approach for improving its reconstruction quality. However, it results in slow convergence for diffusion models, leading to poorer generation quality despite better reconstruction quality. This issue limits the quality upper bound of latent diffusion models and hinders the employment of autoencoders with higher spatial compression ratios. We introduce two key innovations to address this challenge: i) Structured Latent Space, a training-based approach to impose a desired channel-wise structure on the latent space with front latent channels capturing object structures and latter latent channels capturing image details; ii) Augmented Diffusion Training, an augmented diffusion training strategy with additional diffusion training objectives on object latent channels to accelerate convergence. With these techniques, DC-AE 1.5 delivers faster convergence and better diffusion scaling results than DC-AE. On ImageNet 512x512, DC-AE-1.5-f64c128 delivers better image generation quality than DC-AE-f32c32 while being 4x faster. Code: this https URL.
title: DC-AE 1.5: Accelerating Diffusion Model Convergence with Structured Latent Space
